Category.Name: Consistency
Category.Description: Ensures the model adheres to company standards, maintains accuracy across various checks, and minimizes inconsistencies in feature usage and parameter definitions.

Category.Name: Model Complexity
Category.Description: A high, medium, or low complexity score indicates the overall intricacy of the model. Complexity can stem from the number of features, dependencies, or design decisions. While not inherently good or bad, complex models may present maintenance challenges and inefficiencies. Opportunities to simplify models should be evaluated to streamline processes.

Category.Name: Model Integrity
Category.Description: Assesses the structural soundness of the model, ensuring that all features are well-formed, without errors, and that relationships between parts and assemblies are robust.

Category.Name: Design Intent Strength
Category.Description: Measures how effectively the model captures design intent, ensuring that relationships between dimensions, constraints, and features are robust and can handle modifications without failure. Proper design intent facilitates model changes and reduces future engineering costs.

Category.Name: Geometry Quality
Category.Description: Evaluates the mathematical accuracy and validity of the model's geometry, ensuring that it is reliable for downstream processes such as manufacturing, verification, or simulation. Good geometry quality prevents errors from cascading into subsequent stages of product development.

Category.Name: MBD Readiness
Category.Description: Evaluates Product Manufacturing Information (PMI) for Model-Based Definition (MBD) readiness, ensuring annotations, GDT, datums, and manufacturing data are complete, associated, standards-compliant, and usable downstream.

Check.Name: Model Accuracy
Check.Description: Checks and reports the geometric accuracy settings used within the model, including relative or absolute accuracy definitions that control how precisely geometry is calculated and regenerated. Proper accuracy settings are critical for maintaining stable geometry, reliable Boolean operations, successful feature regeneration, accurate mass properties, and high-quality downstream manufacturing, simulation, and data exchange processes. Accuracy values that are too loose can create gaps, failed geometry, and regeneration problems, while excessively tight accuracy can negatively impact performance and model complexity. This check helps ensure that model accuracy settings are appropriate and consistent with company standards and intended model usage. Accuracy limits and related standards are typically configured in the constant.mcn file using settings such as ACCURACY_LOW, ACCURACY_UPP, MIN_ABS_ACCURACY, and MAX_ABS_ACCURACY, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the ACCURACY_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W W W Y
Check.why:
Relative accuracy puts your models at risk for both geometry and regeneration issues. Consider switching to absolute accuracy to avoid the potential issues caused by relative accuracy. Using relative accuracy allows regeneration calculations to execute quickly and use minimal memory. While relative accuracy helps performance, it makes assumptions about the size of the geometry in your model that are sometimes invalid. If the assumptions are invalid, your models may have geom checks and/or regeneration issues that can be fixed by switching to absolute accuracy. This Model Analysis considers relative accuracy a risk because of the strong correlation between relative accuracy and both geometry and regeneration issues. If you do not have either geometry or regeneration issues, then using relative accuracy may be acceptable. You must find an accuracy setting that balances acceptable geometry with regeneration performance, regardless of whether you are using relative or absolute accuracy. The lower the accuracy value, the finer the accuracy. Conversely, the higher the accuracy value, the coarser the accuracy. If the accuracy value is too coarse, the calculated geometry may be inaccurate. If the accuracy value is too fine, however, the memory consumption and regeneration time of your models may become unacceptable. You need to take the geometry factor into account when making your accuracy setting. When your model is regenerated, the accuracy setting you provide gets multiplied by a geometry factor. The geometry factor is typically 3, but can grow to 10 depending on the features of your model. If you are using relative accuracy, you must consider the bounding box diagonal length when making your accuracy settings. **Note** that coarse accuracy settings often lead to inaccurate calculations, but this is not always the case. If you have reviewed the geometry and do not observe geom checks or regeneration issues, then the coarse accuracy setting may be acceptable.

Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Additional Check Parameters
Check.Description: Checks whether additional parameters and metadata have been configured to appear in ModelCHECK reports and validation output. Additional check parameters are commonly used to expose important model information such as materials, units, model names, lifecycle states, custom metadata, and other values that support reporting, metrics, troubleshooting, and downstream automation processes. Properly configured check parameters improve visibility into model quality and help organizations monitor compliance with company standards. This check helps ensure that required reporting parameters are consistently included and available within the ModelCHECK environment. Additional reporting parameters are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_ADD_CHK_PARAM, ASM_ADD_CHK_PARAM, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the ADD_CHK_PARAM settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN N N N N Y

Check.Name: Buried Features
Check.Description: Checks for features that become fully hidden, consumed, or buried inside other geometry where they no longer contribute meaningful visible geometry to the final model. Buried features are often created unintentionally through later cuts, merges, Boolean operations, or overlapping geometry changes, and may indicate poor modeling practices, obsolete design intent, or unnecessary feature complexity. These hidden features can increase regeneration time, complicate troubleshooting, weaken model maintainability, and create confusion during future modifications or downstream manufacturing processes. This check helps identify geometry that may need cleanup or redesign to improve model clarity, stability, and efficiency. Check behavior and reporting are controlled by the BURIED_FEAT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E E E Y
Check.why:
A buried feature has geometry that is entirely surrounded (or enclosed) by another feature’s geometry. For example, a cut feature may be completely filled by one or more features. Or a solid feature, such as a protrusion, may be completely surrounded by geometry from one or more other features. The design intent of a buried feature should be questioned since it is being replaced by geometry from other features. Often buried features indicate a model that was modified when undesired geometry was cut away or an undesired region was filled in to start over. This may feel like a pragmatic solution, but it is only a short term solution. When the undesired design intent is left behind (or buried by geometry from new features), it makes the model unnecessarily complex. Good design intent should be understandable and the designer should make changes by modifying and redesigning the existing design intent. Leaving features that do not contribute to the design intent is confusing for new designers. ### Cuts outside the model: A cut outside the model is a cut feature that does not remove any material. The design intent of a cut that does not remove material should be questioned. Cuts outside the model are often caused by over-built sketches for cuts that intersect and contribute to the model’s geometry. In the current model state these sketches have no effect. You should express the design intent in a way that satisfies all potential input cases, since this ensures each feature has a purpose and that it creates the desired geometry based on the design intent. ### Unattached features: An unattached feature creates solid material that is not connected to the main solid. All parts should have no more than one solid body. If more than one solid body is present, this may indicate that you need an assembly. Note that sometimes unattached features are acceptable, because later features connect the solids so the part has a single solid volume. Similar to cuts outside the model, unattached features are often caused by over-built geometry that sometimes intersects the main solid, although in the current state it does not. Consider redesigning the feature with references such as up to surface so the solid material always intersects the model. Or perhaps you can add a sketch reference that ensures the sketch is attached to the main solid.

Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Chamfer Child Features
Check.Description: Checks for features that depend on chamfer geometry as parent references within the model. Chamfers are typically finishing features added later in the design process, and using them as references for downstream features can weaken design intent and create unstable parent-child relationships. If a chamfer is modified, removed, or reordered, dependent features may fail to regenerate or produce unexpected geometry changes. This check helps encourage more robust and maintainable modeling practices by reducing dependencies on cosmetic or finishing features. Check behavior and reporting are controlled by the CHAMFER_CHILD settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W E E Y
Check.why:
Creating child features that reference chamfered features is problematic because it can lead to unstable models and complicate future design changes. Chamfers should be added later in the model tree to maintain the integrity and flexibility of the design, ensuring that primary features remain robust and modifications are easier to manage.

Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Child Features Existence
Check.Description: Checks whether required child features exist and maintain valid parent-child relationships within the model. Child features are dependent on parent geometry, references, or features for their placement and definition, and missing or broken child relationships can lead to regeneration failures, unstable geometry, and inconsistent model behavior during design changes. Properly managed feature dependencies help maintain strong design intent, predictable updates, and overall model integrity. This check helps identify missing or invalid child feature relationships so models remain stable, maintainable, and compliant with company modeling practices. Parent-child relationship behavior and related standards are managed through model design practices and ModelCHECK configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the CHILDREN_EXIST settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y E E Y

Check.Name: Cosmetic Features
Check.Description: Checks for cosmetic features such as cosmetic threads, cosmetic sketches, cosmetic welds, and other non-solid or annotation-style geometry used to represent manufacturing or design details without creating full physical geometry. While cosmetic features can improve drawing clarity and reduce model complexity, excessive or improperly managed cosmetic geometry can create confusion, reduce model readability, and introduce inconsistencies between the visual representation and actual manufacturable geometry. This check helps identify cosmetic features so they can be reviewed and managed according to company modeling and documentation standards. Cosmetic feature behavior and related standards are managed through Creo modeling practices and ModelCHECK configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the COSMETIC_FEAT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Cylinder Cuts/Slots
Check.Description: Checks for slot or cut features that could be more appropriately modeled as standard cylindrical holes. Using proper hole features instead of manually created cylindrical cuts or slot geometry improves model clarity, manufacturing consistency, hole table support, downstream automation, and compatibility with machining and CAM processes. Nonstandard cut geometry may reduce feature recognition, complicate manufacturing documentation, and make models more difficult to maintain or modify. This check helps encourage the use of standard hole definitions and cleaner modeling practices where appropriate. Hole standards and related geometry definitions are typically configured through ModelCHECK configuration files and external hole definition tables, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the CYL_CUT_SLOTS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Cylindrical Cut/Slot Diameters
Check.Description: Checks whether cylindrical features such as holes, shafts, pins, or round cut geometry use approved or standardized diameter values according to company and manufacturing standards. Controlling cylindrical diameters helps ensure manufacturability, tooling compatibility, standard hardware usage, and consistency across designs. Incorrect, nonstandard, or excessively precise diameters can create machining difficulties, increase manufacturing cost, complicate inspection, and reduce compatibility with standard components and downstream automation processes. This check helps identify cylindrical geometry that may need to be adjusted to approved standard sizes or tolerances. Standard diameter values are typically configured through external hole and diameter definition files referenced by the start.mcs configuration, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the CYL_DIAMS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Datum Feature Renaming
Check.Description: Checks whether datum features such as datum planes, axes, coordinate systems, points, and curves follow approved company naming conventions. Consistent datum naming improves model readability, design communication, feature identification, and long-term maintainability, especially in complex models and assemblies. Poorly named or default datum names can make models difficult to understand, troubleshoot, and modify, and may create confusion during detailing, manufacturing, inspection, and downstream collaboration. This check helps ensure that datum features are clearly and consistently named according to organizational standards. Datum naming rules are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_DATUM_RENAME, ASM_DATUM_RENAME, PRT_DATUM_PLANE, ASM_DATUM_PLANE, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DATUM_RENAME settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Undefined Material Density
Check.Description: Checks whether materials assigned to the model contain valid and properly defined density values used for mass property calculations and downstream analysis. Accurate material density is critical for correct weight calculations, center of gravity results, simulation accuracy, structural analysis, manufacturing estimates, and BOM data. Missing, incorrect, or unrealistic density values can lead to inaccurate mass properties, poor engineering decisions, and downstream manufacturing or performance issues. This check helps ensure that all assigned materials contain appropriate density definitions that comply with company and engineering standards. Material assignments and approved material definitions are typically managed through Creo material libraries and ModelCHECK material settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DEF_DENSITY settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y
Check.why: The model density is necessary to evaluate the mass properties of a solid model. The default density is often 1.0, and it is highly unlikely this is valid for a real material. If your model’s mass properties are important, then the density should be set to the appropriate value of the intended material.

Check.Name: Default Datum Plane Children
Check.Description: Checks whether required default child features created from standard templates, start parts, or predefined modeling practices are present and properly associated within the model. Default child features often include standard datums, reference geometry, coordinate systems, construction features, or other foundational elements that support stable parametric relationships and consistent modeling practices. Missing or improperly managed default child features can weaken design intent, create unstable dependencies, and reduce model consistency across projects and teams. This check helps ensure that models retain the standard feature structure expected by the organization. Default feature definitions and related standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using datum, layer, parameter, and feature-related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DEFAULT_CHILD settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Draft Angles
Check.Description: Checks whether draft features and drafted surfaces use valid and manufacturable draft angle values according to company and manufacturing standards. Proper draft angles are critical for molded, cast, forged, and formed parts because they allow parts to release cleanly from tooling without damage or excessive wear. Insufficient or incorrect draft angles can create manufacturing difficulties, tooling problems, poor surface quality, increased production cost, and part release failures. This check helps identify geometry that may not meet manufacturing requirements or approved design standards for draft. Approved draft angle ranges and standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_PARAMETER draft angle definitions and related external draft angle tables such as STD_DRAFT_ANGLE_FILE entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DRAFT_ANGLES settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Draft angles are crucial in injection molded parts to facilitate easy ejection, reduce mold wear, improve surface finish, maintain part integrity, simplify mold design, lower production costs, enhance part appearance and functionality, and ensure efficient manufacturing with typical angles ranging from 1 to 3 degrees depending on material and part geometry.

Check.Name: Draft Children
Check.Description: Checks for features that depend on draft geometry as parent references. Draft features are often added later in the design process to satisfy manufacturing requirements, and using them as references for downstream features can weaken design intent and create unstable parent-child relationships. Changes to draft geometry may cause dependent features to fail, regenerate incorrectly, or produce unexpected geometry changes during future modifications. This check helps encourage stronger and more maintainable modeling practices by reducing dependencies on manufacturing-specific finishing features. Draft feature standards and related modeling practices are typically configured through ModelCHECK geometry settings and company modeling standards, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DRAFT_CHILD settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why:
Draft features can vary significantly as both their dimensions and their parent’s dimensions change. Because of the potentially significant changes in their geometry, it is unwise to reference their edges or surfaces. Since the internal structure and nature of the draft’s geometry can vary significantly with small changes, referencing the draft’s geometry is unreliable if changes are anticipated. Drafts are similarly complex features whose resulting geometry can vary significantly with small changes.

Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Datum Axes Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to datum axes used within the model. Datum axes are commonly used as reference geometry for holes, patterns, revolved features, assemblies, manufacturing operations, inspection setups, and downstream automation processes. Properly defined and consistently managed datum axes improve model clarity, design intent, feature alignment, and overall maintainability. Missing, poorly defined, or inconsistently named datum axes can create confusion, weaken parametric relationships, and lead to downstream manufacturing or inspection issues. This check helps ensure that datum axes are properly defined and used according to company standards. Datum axis requirements and naming standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_DATUM_AXIS, ASM_DATUM_AXIS, and related datum configuration entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DTM_AXES_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Datum Coordinate System Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to coordinate systems used within the model. Coordinate systems are critical reference elements used for assembly placement, manufacturing setups, CNC programming, simulation, data exchange, robotics, inspection, and integration with downstream systems. Properly defined and consistently managed coordinate systems improve model alignment, interoperability, automation reliability, and communication across engineering workflows. Missing, incorrectly oriented, or poorly named coordinate systems can create assembly positioning errors, manufacturing issues, export problems, and downstream integration failures. This check helps ensure that coordinate systems are properly defined and maintained according to company standards. Coordinate system requirements and naming standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_DATUM_CSYS, ASM_DATUM_CSYS, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DTM_CSYS_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Datum Curve Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to datum curves used within the model. Datum curves are commonly used as reference geometry for sweeps, surfaces, routing, manufacturing paths, layout control, and complex feature creation. Properly defined and managed datum curves improve design intent, feature stability, model readability, and downstream manufacturing or inspection workflows. Poorly constructed, unnecessary, or inconsistently used datum curves can weaken parametric relationships, complicate modifications, and create downstream geometry or manufacturing issues. This check helps ensure that datum curves are properly defined and used according to company modeling standards. Datum curve requirements and related standards are typically configured through ModelCHECK start.mcs definitions and geometry management practices, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DTM_CURVE_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Datum Plane Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to datum planes used within the model. Datum planes are foundational reference features used to define geometry orientation, sketch placement, symmetry, assembly alignment, manufacturing setups, and downstream design intent. Properly defined and consistently managed datum planes improve model stability, readability, maintainability, and communication across engineering workflows. Missing, poorly named, or improperly oriented datum planes can weaken parametric relationships, complicate modifications, and create downstream assembly, manufacturing, or inspection issues. This check helps ensure that datum planes are properly defined and used according to company standards. Datum plane requirements and naming standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_DATUM_PLANE, ASM_DATUM_PLANE, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DTM_PLANE_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Datum Point Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to datum points used within the model. Datum points are commonly used as reference geometry for feature placement, hole locations, coordinate definition, measurement references, manufacturing setups, assembly positioning, and inspection processes. Properly defined and consistently managed datum points improve model clarity, design intent, and downstream workflow reliability. Missing, poorly defined, or inconsistently used datum points can create alignment problems, weak parametric relationships, and downstream manufacturing or inspection issues. This check helps ensure that datum points are properly defined and maintained according to company standards. Datum point requirements and related standards are typically configured through ModelCHECK start.mcs definitions and geometry management practices, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DTM_POINT_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Duplicate Models
Check.Description: Checks for models that may duplicate existing parts or assemblies based on naming, geometry, metadata, or database records. Duplicate models can create confusion, increase maintenance effort, introduce inconsistent revisions, and lead to errors in BOMs, manufacturing, purchasing, Windchill, and downstream data management systems. Maintaining unique and properly controlled models improves traceability, reuse, standardization, and overall database integrity. This check helps identify potentially redundant or duplicate models so they can be reviewed and consolidated according to company data management practices. Duplicate model detection behavior and related standards are controlled through ModelCHECK and data management configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DUPLICATE_MODELS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Early Chamfer Features
Check.Description: Checks for chamfer features that are created too early in the model feature tree before the primary geometry is fully defined. Chamfers are typically finishing features that should be added later in the modeling process after major geometry, functional features, and design intent have been established. Creating chamfers too early can weaken parent-child relationships, increase regeneration instability, complicate future modifications, and cause downstream features to fail when geometry changes occur. This check helps encourage stronger and more maintainable modeling practices by promoting proper feature order and model structure. Check behavior and reporting are controlled by the EARLY_CHAMFER settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why:
Using chamfers too early in the design model tree can lead to significant problems, particularly if they are used as references for constructing other features, because this practice can compromise the integrity of the model, complicate future design modifications, and create dependencies that make the design less flexible and more difficult to manage.

Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Early Cosmetic Features
Check.Description: Checks for cosmetic features that are created too early in the model feature tree before the primary geometry and functional design intent are fully established. Cosmetic features such as cosmetic threads, cosmetic sketches, and annotation-style geometry are typically intended as finishing or documentation features and should generally be added later in the modeling process. Creating cosmetic features too early can complicate parent-child relationships, reduce model clarity, increase regeneration instability, and make future modifications more difficult. This check helps encourage cleaner and more maintainable model structure by promoting proper feature sequencing and organization. Check behavior and reporting are controlled by the EARLY_COSMETIC settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Early Draft Features
Check.Description: Checks for draft features that are created too early in the model feature tree before the primary geometry and core design intent are fully established. Draft features are typically added later in the modeling process to satisfy manufacturing requirements for molded, cast, or formed parts. Creating draft features too early can weaken parent-child relationships, increase regeneration instability, complicate future design changes, and cause downstream features to fail when geometry is modified. This check helps encourage stronger and more maintainable modeling practices by promoting proper feature sequencing and stable model structure. Check behavior and reporting are controlled by the EARLY_DRAFT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why:
Using drafts too early in the design model tree can lead to significant problems, particularly if they are used as references for constructing other features, because this practice can compromise the integrity of the model, complicate future design modifications, and create dependencies that make the design less flexible and more difficult to manage.

Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Early Round Features
Check.Description: Checks for round or fillet features that are created too early in the model feature tree before the primary geometry and core design intent are fully established. Rounds are typically finishing features added later in the modeling process after major geometry, functional features, and manufacturing-critical elements have been completed. Creating rounds too early can weaken parent-child relationships, increase regeneration instability, complicate future modifications, and cause downstream features to fail when geometry changes occur. This check helps encourage more robust and maintainable modeling practices by promoting proper feature sequencing and stable model structure. Check behavior and reporting are controlled by the EARLY_ROUND settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Edge References
Check.Description: Checks for model features that reference edges as parent geometry or placement references. Edge references are often considered weak or unstable because edges can easily change, disappear, split, or reorder when geometry is modified. Features that depend on edges are more likely to fail during regeneration, break after design changes, or create unstable parent-child relationships. Using more stable references such as datum geometry, surfaces, axes, or sketches generally improves model robustness and maintainability. This check helps identify potentially fragile modeling practices that could lead to regeneration problems and difficult future modifications. Edge reference behavior and related modeling standards are managed through company modeling practices and ModelCHECK configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the EDGE_REFERENCES settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W E E Y
Check.why:
Using edge references in a model can lead to significant problems, such as increased susceptibility to errors when geometry changes, difficulty in maintaining design intent, and challenges in updating the model, as edge references can create unstable dependencies that compromise the robustness of the design.
Edge references are not recommended since future changes to the design intent may cause the edge to disappear and in turn, the feature that references the now non existant edge will likely fail. An edge is one type of geometric entity that represents the boundary of an internal surface or face on a solid, or the boundary of a surface or a surface’s internal patch. If an edge disappears, then features that referenced the edge will fail to regenerate. Because of the risk of an edge disappearing, edges are usually considered unstable and weak choices for references.
Always consider alternate reference types before selecting an edge. Usually an alternate reference such as a surface or an intent object are more robust. Ensure you do not accidentally select an edge when another type of reference would be more robust under change. It is a good practice to use the Query Select option when selecting a reference and to carefully observe the selection. Fortunately, beginning with Pro/ENGINEER Wildfire 4.0 surfaces are selected by default, reducing the chance of accidentally selecting an edge.
If you feel an edge reference is appropriate and necessary:
- Understand that it may be unstable under change and you risk experiencing regeneration errors about missing references after making a change. - Limit 1by1 edge selections and instead prefer intent objects or rule based selection such as tangent chain, boundary chain, surface loop, or from-to chain. Intent objects and rule based selections minimize the number of references and can be more robust under change. - Use care when sequencing the order of features in the model tree. For example, round features that reference edges should occur before any feature that may consume edges it references. - When changes are required to a sketch that creates edges that are referenced downstream, ensure you replace the sketched entities rather than simply deleting and adding entities. When the Replace operation is used, downstream references are rerouted automatically.

Check.Name: Extra Layers
Check.Description: Checks for layers that exist in the model but are not part of the approved company layer structure or start model standards. Extra or unnecessary layers can reduce model organization, create confusion, complicate visibility management, and make models more difficult to maintain and standardize across projects and teams. Uncontrolled layers may also interfere with downstream drawing, manufacturing, data exchange, and automation workflows. This check helps ensure that only approved layers are used and that models remain clean, organized, and compliant with company standards. Approved layer definitions are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_LAYER, ASM_LAYER, DRW_LAYER, PRT_LAYER_UNWANTED, ASM_LAYER_UNWANTED, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the EXTRA_LAYERS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why:
Layers are used in models and drawings to control which view items are displayed. Models and drawings with many pieces of information can be difficult to work with if all of the information is turned on at the same time.
By organizing view items into different layers, you can select which layers are displayed, hidden, or isolated so you can focus on the desired information. Understanding the number of layers in your models and the occurrence of layers without entities is a good starting point for assessing how effectively you are using layers. A high level view of how layers are being used is also helpful for very specialized types of CAD data conversion projects.
**Note:** Layers can only organize view items such as datums, surface quilts, and components. The hiding or isolation of specific features within a part’s solid or within a surface’s quilt is not possible with layers. Instead, simplified representations should be created to control the presence of specific features.

Check.Name: Non-Start-Part Parameters
Check.Description: Checks for parameters that exist in the model but are not part of the approved company parameter standards or required metadata definitions. Extra or unnecessary parameters can increase model complexity, create confusion, introduce inconsistent metadata, and interfere with automation, drawings, Windchill integration, BOM generation, ERP mapping, and downstream manufacturing processes. Obsolete or duplicate parameters may also make models more difficult to maintain and troubleshoot over time. This check helps ensure that models contain only approved and necessary parameter data according to company standards. Approved and unwanted parameter definitions are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_PARAMETER, ASM_PARAMETER, DRW_PARAMETER, PRT_PARAM_UNWANTED, ASM_PARAM_UNWANTED, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the EXTRA_PARAMS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why:
Including extra parameters in a model can lead to significant problems, such as increased complexity, potential for errors, confusion during modifications, and difficulties in maintaining the model, all of which can hinder efficient design and development.

Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Family Table Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to family tables used within the model. Family tables are commonly used to create configurable variations of parts or assemblies from a single generic model by controlling dimensions, parameters, features, materials, and other design options. Properly managed family tables improve standardization, reuse, automation, and design efficiency while reducing duplicate models and maintenance effort. Poorly structured or inconsistent family tables can create configuration errors, incorrect BOM data, regeneration problems, and downstream manufacturing or data management issues. This check helps ensure that family table information is properly defined and maintained according to company standards. Family table requirements and related standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_FT_PARAMETER and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the FAMILY_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Feature Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information about features, surface features, reference geometry, patterns, manufacturing features, and other parametric elements that define the model structure. Properly managed features improve design intent, model stability, readability, maintainability, and downstream manufacturing and inspection workflows. Poorly organized, unnecessary, or unstable features can increase model complexity, create regeneration problems, and make future modifications more difficult. This check helps provide visibility into feature usage and overall model structure to support company modeling standards and quality practices. Feature-related standards and configuration settings are typically managed through the start.mcs file and related ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the FEATURE_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: File Size
Check.Description: Checks whether the model file size falls within acceptable limits defined by company standards and performance guidelines. Excessively large model files can negatively impact regeneration time, assembly performance, drawing loading, data management operations, collaboration, and downstream manufacturing or simulation workflows. Large file sizes are often caused by overly complex geometry, excessive feature counts, imported geometry issues, unnecessary detail, or inefficient modeling practices. This check helps identify models that may require simplification, cleanup, or optimization to improve performance, maintainability, and storage efficiency. File size thresholds and related limits are typically configured in the constant.mcn file using file size and model complexity settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the FILE_SIZE settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Freeform Features
Check.Description: Checks for the presence and use of freeform or freestyle geometry features within the model. Freeform features are commonly used to create complex organic shapes, ergonomic surfaces, aesthetic geometry, and advanced surface designs that cannot easily be created with standard parametric features. While freeform geometry can provide significant design flexibility, excessive or poorly controlled freeform features can increase model complexity, reduce maintainability, complicate downstream manufacturing, and create challenges for modification, regeneration, simulation, and geometry translation. This check helps identify freeform geometry usage so it can be reviewed and managed according to company modeling and manufacturing standards. Freeform feature usage and related standards are managed through company modeling practices and ModelCHECK configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the FREEFORM settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Family Table Default Values
Check.Description: Checks whether feature templates use the correct default values defined by company modeling standards. Feature templates are commonly used to standardize frequently used geometry, manufacturing features, annotations, and design practices across models and projects. Incorrect or inconsistent default values can lead to nonstandard geometry, improper manufacturing settings, incorrect metadata, and downstream automation or documentation issues. This check helps ensure that template-driven features are created with approved starting values and consistent configuration behavior. Feature template standards and default value definitions are typically managed through Creo template configuration files and ModelCHECK setup definitions, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the FT_DEF_VALS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Feature Template Standard Parameters
Check.Description: Checks whether feature templates contain the required standard parameters and approved metadata defined by company modeling standards. Feature templates are commonly used to create consistent geometry, manufacturing features, annotations, and reusable design elements across models and projects. Missing, incorrect, or inconsistent template parameters can lead to nonstandard models, failed automation, incorrect manufacturing data, and downstream documentation or Windchill integration issues. This check helps ensure that feature templates include the proper standard parameters needed to support consistent modeling practices and downstream processes. Standard feature template parameters are typically configured through Creo template definitions and ModelCHECK parameter standards in the start.mcs file, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the FT_STD_PARMS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Geometric Checks
Check.Description: Checks the model for geometric errors, invalid geometry, and conditions that may affect regeneration, manufacturability, simulation accuracy, or downstream data exchange. Geometric problems can include failed surfaces, gaps, overlaps, self-intersecting geometry, sliver surfaces, inconsistent topology, imported geometry defects, and other conditions that weaken model integrity and stability. Poor geometric quality can lead to regeneration failures, machining issues, inaccurate mass properties, failed simulations, export problems, and manufacturing defects. This check helps identify geometry that may require repair, simplification, or cleanup to ensure robust and reliable model behavior. Geometry quality limits and related standards are typically configured through the constant.mcn file and other ModelCHECK geometry configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the GEOM_CHECKS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N E Y
Check.why:
Geom checks should be removed from all models that will be reused or referenced in downstream work. Sometimes resolving geom checks causes new geom checks or regeneration issues to appear. This occurs when the original geom check masked or cloaked downstream problems. If this is the case, it can be difficult and potentially impractical to fix the geom checks if they went unnoticed or were ignored for a long time. This is why it is important to resolve geom checks sooner rather than later. Geom checks may propagate through the dependency chain Models or drawings that reference models with geom checks risk inheriting a similar geometry issue that can become more serious. Even if the model with a geom check is a distant ancestor and not an immediate parent, it may be possible for the geom check to skip a generation and propagate to the child or grandchild. ### Geom checks are often caused by: - Regeneration issues - Inappropriate model accuracy settings - Import features - Merge or cutout features - External copy geometry features - Copy geometry features ### Resolving geom checks Since an issue may propagate through the associative chain of references between models, first focus on the models at the top of the dependency chain. Beginning with the isolated occurrences is a good strategy because fixing them may also resolve the geom checks in their downstream models.

Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Geometric Tolerances
Check.Description: Checks whether geometric tolerances are properly defined and applied to features within the model according to engineering and manufacturing requirements. Geometric tolerances control allowable variation in form, orientation, location, and profile to ensure that parts assemble correctly, function as intended, and can be manufactured and inspected accurately. Missing, incorrect, or inconsistent geometric tolerances can lead to fitment problems, inspection failures, manufacturing defects, and downstream quality issues. This check helps ensure that tolerance definitions comply with company drafting and manufacturing standards. Approved geometric tolerance standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_TOL_TYPE, ASM_TOL_TYPE, and related tolerance configuration entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the GEOMETRIC_TOL settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Hole Diameters
Check.Description: Checks whether hole features use approved or standardized diameter values according to company and manufacturing standards. Standard hole diameters improve manufacturability, tooling compatibility, hardware fitment, inspection consistency, and downstream automation processes such as hole tables, CNC programming, and CAM operations. Incorrect, nonstandard, or excessively precise hole diameters can increase manufacturing cost, create machining difficulties, and reduce compatibility with standard tooling and fasteners. This check helps identify hole geometry that may need to be adjusted to approved standard sizes or tolerances. Approved hole diameter values are typically configured in external standard files referenced by the start.mcs file using STD_HOLE_DIAM_FILE entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the HOLE_DIAMS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Ignored Features
Check.Description: Checks for features that have been marked to be ignored or excluded from certain ModelCHECK evaluations, regeneration behavior, or reporting processes. Ignored features may be intentionally excluded for temporary design work, imported geometry handling, legacy model support, or special modeling situations, but excessive or unnecessary ignored features can reduce model transparency, hide potential problems, and make models more difficult to maintain and troubleshoot. Improper use of ignored features may also allow modeling issues to bypass standard validation processes and create downstream manufacturing, assembly, or documentation risks. This check helps identify ignored features so they can be reviewed and managed according to company modeling standards and quality practices. Check behavior and reporting are controlled by the IGNORE_FEAT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N Y Y

Check.Name: Imported Features
Check.Description: Checks for geometry or features that were created by importing external CAD data from formats such as STEP, IGES, Parasolid, SAT, or other neutral file types. Imported features can introduce geometry quality problems, missing design intent, unstable references, translation errors, excessive complexity, and downstream manufacturing or regeneration issues because imported data often lacks native parametric feature history. Poorly managed imported geometry may also create difficulties during modification, simulation, assembly integration, and data exchange workflows. This check helps identify imported features so they can be reviewed, repaired, simplified, or converted according to company modeling standards and downstream process requirements. Imported geometry behavior and related standards are managed through Creo import settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the IMPORT_FEAT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Incomplete Features
Check.Description: Checks for features that are partially defined, failed, unresolved, or missing required references needed to fully regenerate and function correctly within the model. Incomplete features can result from interrupted feature creation, missing geometry references, failed imports, broken dependencies, or invalid modeling operations. These features can weaken model integrity, create regeneration failures, produce unstable geometry, and lead to downstream problems in assemblies, manufacturing, simulation, drawings, and data exchange processes. This check helps identify incomplete or invalid features so they can be corrected, completed, or removed to maintain stable and reliable model behavior. Check behavior and reporting are controlled by the INCOMPLETE_FEAT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y

Check.Name: Insert Mode On
Check.Description: Checks whether the model is currently using Insert Mode or contains features affected by Insert Mode operations within the feature tree. Insert Mode allows users to temporarily roll back the model history to add, modify, suppress, or troubleshoot features earlier in the regeneration sequence, but improper or unfinished use of Insert Mode can create unstable models, incomplete geometry, suppressed downstream features, and unexpected regeneration behavior. Leaving models in Insert Mode may also cause confusion, hidden geometry changes, and downstream manufacturing or documentation issues. This check helps ensure that Insert Mode is used intentionally and that models are fully regenerated and restored to a stable state before release or downstream use. Check behavior and reporting are controlled by the INSERT_MODE settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y

Check.Name: Instance Naming
Check.Description: Checks whether family table instances and configurable model instances follow approved company naming conventions and naming patterns. Consistent instance naming improves traceability, configuration management, BOM accuracy, searchability, and communication across design, manufacturing, Windchill, and downstream data management systems. Poorly named or inconsistent instances can create confusion between generics and instances, lead to incorrect part usage, and introduce errors in assemblies, drawings, and manufacturing documentation. This check helps ensure that all model instances are clearly and consistently named according to organizational standards. Instance naming rules are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_INSTANCE_NAME, ASM_INSTANCE_NAME, and related family table configuration entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the INSTANCE_NAME settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Layer Display Status
Check.Description: Checks whether layers within the model use the correct visibility and display status settings according to company standards. Layer display states control whether geometry, datums, annotations, curves, axes, and other model entities are shown, hidden, or managed for different workflows such as modeling, detailing, manufacturing, and inspection. Incorrect layer display settings can create visual clutter, hide important geometry, reduce model readability, and cause downstream drawing or manufacturing issues. This check helps ensure that layers are organized and displayed consistently to improve model clarity and maintainability. Layer definitions and approved display states are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_LAYER, ASM_LAYER, DRW_LAYER, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the LAYER_DISPSTAT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y W W Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Multiple Layer Items
Check.Description: Checks whether model items such as dimensions, annotations, datums, curves, axes, surfaces, features, and other entities are placed on the correct layers according to company standards. Proper layer organization improves model readability, visibility control, drawing management, manufacturing workflows, and downstream automation processes. Items placed on incorrect or multiple layers can create confusion, clutter, inconsistent display behavior, and difficulties during detailing, inspection, and data exchange. This check helps ensure that model entities are consistently organized using the approved layer structure defined by the organization. Layer definitions and item assignment standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_LAYER, ASM_LAYER, DRW_LAYER, PRT_LAYER_MOVE, ASM_LAYER_MOVE, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the LAYER_ITEMS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W W W Y
Check.why:
Layers are used in models and drawings to control which view items are displayed. Models and drawings with many pieces of information can be difficult to work with if all of the information is turned on at the same time. By organizing view items into different layers, you can select which layers are displayed, hidden, or isolated so you can focus on the desired information. Understanding the number of layers in your models and the occurrence of layers without entities is a good starting point for assessing how effectively you are using layers. A high level view of how layers are being used is also helpful for very specialized types of CAD data conversion projects. Note: Layers can only organize view items such as datums, surface quilts, and components. The hiding or isolation of specific features within a part’s solid or within a surface’s quilt is not possible with layers. Instead, simplified representations should be created to control the presence of specific features.

Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Datum Features on Unblanked Layers
Check.Description: Checks whether datum-related layers are properly configured to use the correct blanked or hidden display state according to company standards. Datum layers commonly contain datum planes, axes, coordinate systems, points, and other reference geometry that may clutter the model display if left visible during normal modeling and detailing workflows. Properly blanking datum layers improves model readability, reduces visual complexity, and helps maintain consistent display practices across parts, assemblies, and drawings. Incorrect datum layer visibility settings can make models difficult to navigate and may interfere with downstream drafting, manufacturing, or inspection processes. Datum layer definitions and approved display states are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_LAYER, ASM_LAYER, DRW_LAYER, and related layer configuration entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the LAYER_DTM_BLANK settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y W W Y

Check.Name: External Items in Layers

External items may include copied geometry, imported curves, external references, shared data, or linked entities originating from outside the current model. Poorly managed external layer content can create confusion, weaken model maintainability, complicate visibility management, and introduce downstream issues during regeneration, data exchange, manufacturing, or collaboration workflows. This check helps identify externally sourced items that may require review, cleanup, or standardization to maintain organized and reliable layer structure. Layer organization standards and external item management practices are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_LAYER, ASM_LAYER, DRW_LAYER, and related layer configuration entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the LAYER_EXT_ITEMS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y W W Y

Poorly managed layers can create visual clutter, inconsistent display behavior, downstream drawing issues, and difficulties in manufacturing, inspection, and collaboration processes. This check helps ensure that layers are properly defined, organized, and maintained according to company standards. Layer definitions and standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_LAYER, ASM_LAYER, DRW_LAYER, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the LAYER_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.

Check.Name: Layer Movement
Check.Description: Checks whether model items are automatically moved to the correct layers according to company layer management standards. Layer movement rules are commonly used to organize datums, curves, annotations, surfaces, and other entities into standardized layers to improve model readability, visibility control, downstream drawing management, and manufacturing workflows. Incorrect or inconsistent layer assignment can create visual clutter, reduce organization, and make models more difficult to maintain and interpret. This check helps ensure that model entities are consistently organized using approved layer movement and cleanup rules. Layer movement definitions are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_LAYER_MOVE, ASM_LAYER_MOVE, DRW_LAYER_MOVE, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the LAYER_MOVE settings in the check.mch configuration file.

Check.Name: Layer Placement
Check.Description: Checks whether layers and their associated model entities are placed and organized according to company-defined layer structure and modeling standards. Proper layer placement improves model readability, visibility management, drawing organization, manufacturing workflows, and downstream automation processes by ensuring that datums, annotations, curves, surfaces, construction geometry, and other entities are consistently organized. Incorrectly placed layers or improperly assigned entities can create visual clutter, inconsistent display behavior, and difficulties during design review, detailing, inspection, and manufacturing. This check helps maintain clean, standardized, and well-organized models across projects and teams. Layer placement standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_LAYER, ASM_LAYER, DRW_LAYER, and related layer management entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the LAYER_PLACE settings in the check.mch configuration file.

Check.Name: Layer Status
Check.Description: Checks whether layers within the model have the correct status and behavior according to company layer management standards. Layer status controls how layers behave in the model, including visibility, blanking, activation, and organizational state for datums, annotations, curves, surfaces, construction geometry, and other entities. Layer status definitions and standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_LAYER, ASM_LAYER, DRW_LAYER, and related layer configuration entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the LAYER_STATUS settings in the check.mch configuration file.

Check.Name: Layout Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to layouts used within the model. Layouts are commonly used to define high-level design intent, control geometry placement, manage top-down design relationships, and coordinate dimensions, references, and assembly structure across multiple models. Properly managed layouts improve consistency, parametric control, collaboration, and downstream manufacturing or assembly workflows. Poorly organized or unstable layouts can create weak external references, regeneration problems, dependency issues, and difficulties during future design modifications. This check helps ensure that layouts are properly defined and maintained according to company modeling standards. Layout-related standards and external reference management practices are typically configured through Creo layout settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the LAYOUT_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Lower Tolerance Limits
Check.Description: Checks whether feature dimensions and tolerances use valid and acceptable lower tolerance limits according to company engineering and manufacturing standards. Proper lower tolerance definitions are critical for ensuring part fit, function, manufacturability, inspection accuracy, and assembly performance. Incorrect, excessively tight, or unrealistic lower tolerance limits can increase manufacturing cost, create inspection difficulties, cause fitment problems, and lead to rejected or nonfunctional parts. This check helps identify tolerance definitions that may violate company standards or create downstream production and quality issues. Tolerance standards and approved tolerance types are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_TOL_TYPE, ASM_TOL_TYPE, and related tolerance configuration entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the LOW_TOLERANCE settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Material Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to materials assigned to the model, including material names, material properties, density definitions, and associated manufacturing or engineering metadata. Properly managed material information is critical for accurate mass properties, simulation results, manufacturing processes, BOM generation, cost estimation, and downstream integration with Windchill, ERP, and inspection systems. Missing, incorrect, or inconsistent material assignments can lead to inaccurate calculations, manufacturing errors, poor analysis results, and downstream documentation problems. This check helps ensure that materials are properly assigned and comply with company-approved standards and material libraries. Approved material names and related material standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using MATERIAL_NAME entries and related material configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the MATERIAL_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y

Check.Name: Memory Space Usage
Check.Description: Checks whether the model consumes excessive memory resources during loading, regeneration, or operation within Creo. High memory usage is often caused by overly complex geometry, excessive feature counts, large assemblies, imported geometry, dense patterns, detailed surfacing, or inefficient modeling practices. Models with excessive memory requirements can reduce system performance, increase load and regeneration times, create instability in large assemblies, and negatively affect downstream manufacturing, simulation, and data management workflows. This check helps identify models that may require simplification, optimization, or cleanup to improve performance and maintainability. Memory usage limits and related model complexity standards are typically configured through ModelCHECK constant and performance configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the MEMORY_SPACE settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Merged or Cutout Features
Check.Description: Checks for the presence and use of merge or Boolean merge features within the model. Merge features are commonly used to combine separate bodies, imported geometry, quilts, or component geometry into a single solid or unified geometry definition. While merge operations can be useful for simplifying geometry and integrating external data, excessive or poorly managed merge features can weaken design intent, complicate feature history, reduce model readability, and create regeneration or geometry quality problems. Improper merge operations may also introduce hidden geometry defects, unstable references, and downstream manufacturing or simulation issues. This check helps identify merge feature usage so it can be reviewed and managed according to company modeling standards and best practices. Merge feature behavior and related standards are managed through Creo modeling practices and ModelCHECK configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the MERGE_FEAT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N E Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Min Max Linear Tolerance
Check.Description: Checks whether feature dimensions and tolerances use valid minimum and maximum tolerance values according to company engineering and manufacturing standards. Proper tolerance ranges are critical for ensuring part fit, function, manufacturability, interchangeability, and inspection accuracy. Incorrect, inconsistent, or unrealistic tolerance limits can increase manufacturing cost, create assembly problems, lead to inspection failures, and reduce overall product quality. This check helps identify dimensions and tolerance definitions that may violate approved engineering practices or downstream manufacturing requirements. Tolerance standards and approved tolerance types are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_TOL_TYPE, ASM_TOL_TYPE, and related tolerance configuration entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the MINMAXTOL_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Model Naming
Check.Description: Checks whether model names comply with company-defined naming standards and approved naming patterns. Consistent model naming improves organization, traceability, searchability, and communication across design, manufacturing, and data management systems. Invalid or inconsistent names can lead to duplicate models, revision confusion, and downstream errors in drawings, BOMs, Windchill, and ERP systems. Naming validation rules are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_MODEL_NAME and ASM_MODEL_NAME entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the MODEL_NAME settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N W Y

Check.Name: Model Name Structure
Check.Description: Reports and evaluates the structure and formatting of model names used within the model. This check is primarily used for reporting, metrics, and identifying inconsistent naming patterns rather than directly enforcing naming compliance. Properly structured model names improve readability, traceability, searchability, and consistency across assemblies, drawings, Windchill, and downstream systems. Inconsistent naming structures can make models more difficult to organize, track, and manage throughout the product lifecycle. Naming structure rules and approved naming patterns are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_MODEL_NAME and ASM_MODEL_NAME entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the MODEL_NAME_STR settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Named Features
Check.Description: Checks whether important model features have meaningful user-defined names instead of default or generic feature names. Properly named features improve model readability, maintainability, troubleshooting, collaboration, and long-term supportability by making design intent easier to understand throughout the feature tree. Unnamed or poorly named features can make complex models difficult to edit, review, debug, and modify during future design changes. This check helps encourage consistent feature naming practices that align with company modeling standards and improve overall model clarity. Feature naming behavior and reporting are controlled by the NAMED_FEAT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Model Overall Size
Check.Description: Checks whether the overall physical size of the model falls within defined limits or expected design ranges according to company and manufacturing standards. Monitoring overall model size helps identify parts that may be too large, too small, incorrectly scaled, or outside assembly, packaging, machining, transportation, or manufacturing constraints. Incorrect overall size can lead to fitment problems, tooling issues, assembly interference, excessive material usage, and downstream manufacturing or inspection errors. This check helps ensure that models remain within acceptable dimensional boundaries for their intended application. Overall size limits and related geometry standards are typically configured through ModelCHECK constant and geometry configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the OVERALL_SIZE settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Parameter Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to model parameters used within the part, assembly, or drawing. Parameters are commonly used to store critical metadata such as part numbers, descriptions, materials, project information, lifecycle states, and manufacturing data. Missing, incorrect, or inconsistent parameter information can cause problems in drawings, BOMs, Windchill, ERP integration, manufacturing, and downstream automation processes. This check helps verify that required parameter data exists and is used consistently according to company standards. Parameter requirements and validation rules are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_PARAMETER, ASM_PARAMETER, and DRW_PARAMETER entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the PARAM_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Required Parameter Notes
Check.Description: Checks whether required parameter notes are present and correctly associated with the model or drawing. Parameter notes are commonly used to display important metadata such as part numbers, materials, descriptions, revisions, or manufacturing information on drawings and related documentation. Missing or incorrect parameter notes can lead to incomplete drawings, communication issues, and downstream manufacturing or inspection errors. This check helps ensure that required parameter-driven notes are consistently included according to company standards. Required parameter note definitions are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_PARAM_NOTE_REQ, ASM_PARAM_NOTE_REQ, or DRW_PARAM_NOTE_REQ entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the PARAM_NOTE_REQ settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Unacceptable Parameter Notes
Check.Description: Checks for parameter-driven notes that are not allowed by company standards. Unacceptable parameter notes may include obsolete information, prohibited text, duplicate metadata, incorrect formatting, or notes that conflict with approved drawing and documentation practices. Invalid parameter notes can create confusion, introduce manufacturing or inspection errors, and reduce drawing consistency and data quality. This check helps ensure that only approved parameter notes are used within models and drawings. Unacceptable parameter note definitions are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_PARAM_NOTE_UNACC, ASM_PARAM_NOTE_UNACC, or DRW_PARAM_NOTE_UNACC entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the PARAM_NOTE_UNACC settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Parameter Renaming
Check.Description: Checks whether parameter names comply with company naming standards and identifies parameters that should be renamed to approved names. Standardized parameter naming improves model consistency, automation reliability, searchability, and integration with drawings, Windchill, ERP systems, and downstream processes. Incorrect or inconsistent parameter names can lead to duplicate metadata, failed automation, mapping issues, and confusion across design teams. Parameter rename rules are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_PARAM_RENAME, ASM_PARAM_RENAME, and DRW_PARAM_RENAME entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the PARAM_RENAME settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N Y Y

Check.Name: Parameter Spelling
Check.Description: Checks parameter names and related parameter text for spelling issues that may violate company standards or introduce inconsistent metadata. Misspelled parameter names can cause automation failures, incorrect mappings, reporting issues, search problems, and confusion across design, manufacturing, Windchill, and ERP systems. Consistent spelling improves data quality, model maintainability, and downstream process reliability. Parameter spelling validation may use configured dictionaries, approved lists, or naming standards defined within the ModelCHECK configuration environment, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the PARAM_SPELL settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y
Check.why: Incorrect parameter spelling in a model can lead to significant problems, such as misinterpretation of design intent, errors in calculations or automated processes, and increased difficulty in maintaining and updating the model, all of which can compromise the overall integrity and functionality of the design.

Check.Name: Unused Parameters
Check.Description: Checks a model for parameters that exist but are not being used or are not part of the approved set of parameters defined by company standards. It helps identify unnecessary, obsolete, or redundant parameters that add clutter and can lead to confusion or maintenance issues. ModelCHECK reports these unused parameters so they can be reviewed and removed, improving model quality and consistency.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why:
Unused parameters in a model can lead to confusion, increased complexity, and potential errors during the design process. Ensuring that all parameters are necessary and utilized helps maintain model clarity, simplifies modifications, and enhances the overall efficiency and accuracy of the design.

Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Parameter Checks
Check.Description: Checks whether required model parameters are present, correctly defined, properly formatted, and compliant with company standards. Parameters are commonly used to store critical design and business information such as part numbers, materials, descriptions, lifecycle states, and manufacturing data. Missing, invalid, or incorrectly formatted parameters can lead to errors in drawings, BOMs, Windchill, ERP integration, automation workflows, manufacturing, and downstream documentation processes. This check helps ensure that parameter data is complete, accurate, and consistently applied throughout the model. Parameter validation requirements are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_PARAMETER, ASM_PARAMETER, and DRW_PARAMETER entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the PARAMCHECK settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N W Y

Check.Name: Datum Planes without Children
Check.Description: Checks whether datum planes have dependent child features that reference them within the model. Datum planes that are unused or improperly referenced may indicate unnecessary construction geometry, weak design intent, or poorly organized model structure. Ensuring that datum planes are meaningfully used helps improve model stability, maintainability, and clarity while supporting robust parametric behavior during regeneration and future modifications. Plane usage expectations and related standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_DATUM_PLANE and ASM_DATUM_PLANE entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the PLANE_CHILD settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Plane Parent Features
Check.Description: Checks whether datum planes are properly used as parent references for downstream features within the model. Datum planes often serve as foundational references that control feature placement, orientation, symmetry, and overall design intent. Poorly defined or unstable parent plane references can weaken model robustness, increase regeneration failures, and make future modifications more difficult. This check helps ensure that datum planes are used appropriately to support stable and maintainable parametric relationships. Datum plane standards and naming requirements are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_DATUM_PLANE and ASM_DATUM_PLANE entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the PLANE_PARENT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...
