Check.Name: Last saved Version
Check.Description: Checks the Creo version or build information associated with the model to help identify outdated, legacy, or incompatible files. Models created or last saved in older Creo versions may contain deprecated features, outdated data structures, regeneration differences, or compatibility issues that can affect performance, stability, and downstream processes. Monitoring model version information helps organizations maintain consistent CAD environments and supports upgrade, migration, and data management efforts. Version requirements are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_PRO_VERSION and ASM_PRO_VERSION entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the PRO_VERSION settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y W W Y

Check.Name: Incomplete References
Check.Description: Checks for incomplete or unresolved references within the model that may result from missing geometry, failed dependencies, broken external references, or partially defined features. Incomplete references can weaken model stability, cause regeneration failures, create unpredictable behavior during design changes, and lead to downstream issues in assemblies, drawings, manufacturing, and data management processes. Identifying and correcting incomplete references helps maintain robust parametric relationships and improves overall model integrity and reliability. This check is controlled by the RC_INCOMPLETED settings in the check.mch configuration file and may be influenced by RuleCHECK or related reference-management configurations used within the ModelCHECK environment.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y

Check.Name: Regular Features
Check.Description: Reports the number of resumed (active) features in the model. Suppressed features are excluded from the count. This is an informational metric that provides an indication of model size and feature complexity for reporting, analysis, and quality metrics. REG_FEATURES does not verify the presence of required features or compare the model against company standards defined in the start.mcs file; it simply reports the total number of active features.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Regeneration Errors
Check.Description: Checks for features or model geometry that fail to regenerate correctly during model updates or rebuild operations. Regeneration errors can occur when references are missing, dimensions become invalid, parent-child relationships fail, or design changes create conflicting geometry. These errors can make models unstable, prevent assemblies and drawings from updating properly, and cause downstream manufacturing, simulation, or data management failures. Identifying and resolving regeneration errors helps maintain robust parametric behavior and reliable model performance during future modifications. Check behavior and reporting are controlled by the REGEN_ERRS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y
Check.why:
Regeneration errors in a model are far more severe than warnings, as they can halt the design process entirely, cause loss of data, create significant errors in the model, and necessitate extensive troubleshooting and corrections, severely impacting the reliability and progress of the design workflow. Regeneration issues in a model can cause **significant problems**, such as disrupting the design process, introducing errors, reducing model reliability, and complicating troubleshooting efforts, all of which can hinder the efficiency and accuracy of the overall design workflow. Retrieval and regeneration messages should be reviewed carefully because of their potential impact in downstream use. Note that it is possible for issues to skip a generation and propagate to a model’s grandchildren or more distant descendants, while the model’s direct children appear to have no issues at all. Some types of retrieval and regeneration issues are more serious than others. Consider how you will use the model in downstream work when deciding whether to fix its retrieval and regeneration issues. Retrieval and regeneration issues in models with family tables are more serious than those in non-family table models because will propagate to each instance in the family. ### Types of regeneration issues: - Regeneration failed - Failed relations - Missing and invalid references - Circular references - Design intent unclear warnings - Model changed since mass properties calculated - Frozen components - Incomplete features or components - Missing models - Design changes in upstream models and/or features - Selection of unstable/unreliable references - Geometry issues with referenced geometry - General error messages

Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Regeneration Warnings
Check.Description: Checks for warnings generated during model regeneration that may indicate unstable geometry, weak references, questionable feature behavior, or potential future regeneration failures. Although regeneration warnings do not always stop the model from rebuilding successfully, they can signal underlying problems that may lead to instability, failed updates, or downstream issues in assemblies, drawings, manufacturing, or automation processes. Monitoring and resolving regeneration warnings helps improve model robustness, maintainability, and long-term reliability during design changes. Check behavior and reporting are controlled by the REGEN_WRNS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y
Check.why:
Regeneration issues in a model can cause significant problems, such as disrupting the design process, introducing errors, reducing model reliability, and complicating troubleshooting efforts, all of which can hinder the efficiency and accuracy of the overall design workflow.
Retrieval and regeneration messages should be reviewed carefully because of their potential impact in downstream use. Note that it is possible for issues to skip a generation and propagate to a model’s grandchildren or more distant descendants, while the model’s direct children appear to have no issues at all.
Some types of retrieval and regeneration issues are more serious than others. Consider how you will use the model in downstream work when deciding whether to fix its retrieval and regeneration issues. Retrieval and regeneration issues in models with family tables are more serious than those in non-family table models because will propagate to each instance in the family.
### Types of regeneration issues: - Regeneration failed - Failed relations - Missing and invalid references - Circular references - Design intent unclear warnings - Model changed since mass properties calculated - Frozen components - Incomplete features or components - General error messages

Check.Name: Relations without Comments
Check.Description: Checks whether relations within the model include meaningful comments or documentation to explain their purpose and behavior. Relations often control important parametric logic, calculations, feature behavior, and automation within a model, and undocumented relations can make models difficult to understand, troubleshoot, or modify over time. Adding clear comments improves readability, maintainability, collaboration, and long-term support of complex parametric designs. Relation definitions and standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_RELATION, ASM_RELATION, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the RELATION_COMM settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Relation Errors
Check.Description: Checks for errors, failures, or invalid logic within model relations that may prevent the model from regenerating correctly or behaving as intended. Relation errors can result from incorrect syntax, missing parameters, invalid references, conflicting equations, or failed calculations, and may lead to unstable geometry, regeneration failures, incorrect dimensions, or downstream manufacturing and automation issues. Identifying and correcting relation errors helps maintain robust parametric behavior, reliable model updates, and clear design intent. Relation definitions and standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_RELATION, ASM_RELATION, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the RELATION_ERRS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y

Check.Name: Relation Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to relations used within the model, including parametric logic, equations, and feature-driving rules that control model behavior. Relations are often critical to design intent, automation, calculations, and feature interaction, and poorly managed relations can make models difficult to understand, troubleshoot, or modify. This check helps improve model clarity, maintainability, and consistency by exposing relation usage and related information for review. Relation definitions and standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_RELATION, ASM_RELATION, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the RELATION_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Missing Relations
Check.Description: Checks for required relations that are missing from the model. Relations are commonly used to control parametric behavior, automate calculations, drive dimensions, manage feature behavior, and maintain important design intent within a model. Missing relations can cause incorrect geometry, broken automation, inconsistent behavior, regeneration problems, or downstream manufacturing and documentation issues. This check helps ensure that all required relations are present and functioning as intended according to company modeling standards. Required relation definitions are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_RELATION, ASM_RELATION, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the RELATION_MISS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Multiple Relations
Check.Description: Checks for parameters or dimensions that are assigned multiple or conflicting relations within the model. Excessive or overlapping relations can make models difficult to understand, troubleshoot, and maintain, especially when multiple equations attempt to control the same behavior or design intent. Poorly organized relations may lead to unexpected geometry changes, regeneration instability, calculation conflicts, and confusion during future modifications. This check helps identify overly complex or redundant relation logic so models remain clear, stable, and maintainable. Relation definitions and standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_RELATION, ASM_RELATION, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the RELATION_MULT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W W W Y
Check.why: Similar to an equation in a spreadsheet, a relation is used to perform calculations and assign the result to a parameter or dimension of the model. Having many relations can lead to poor design intent if not well documented.

Check.Name: Renamed Symbols
Check.Description: Checks for drawing or annotation symbols that have been renamed from their original or standard names. Renamed symbols can create inconsistencies in drawings, reduce standardization, and make models and documentation more difficult to interpret, maintain, or automate. Unexpected symbol names may also cause issues with downstream processes such as detailing, inspection, manufacturing documentation, and company drafting standards. This check helps ensure that symbols remain consistent and compliant with approved naming practices. Symbol naming standards and approved symbols are typically configured in the start.mcs file using DRW_SYMBOL and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the RENAMED_SYMBOLS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Round Child Features
Check.Description: Checks whether other features are dependent on round features within the model. Features that reference rounds as parent geometry can weaken design intent and create unstable parent-child relationships because rounds are often added late in the modeling process and may change frequently during design revisions. When downstream features depend on rounds, modifications to the round can cause regeneration failures, broken references, or unexpected geometry changes. This check helps encourage stronger and more maintainable modeling practices by reducing dependencies on cosmetic or finishing features. Check behavior and reporting are controlled by the ROUND_CHILD settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W E E Y
Check.why:
Round features can vary significantly as both their dimensions and their parent’s dimensions change. Because of the potentially significant changes in their geometry, it is unwise to reference their edges or surfaces. Since the internal structure and nature of the round’s geometry can vary significantly with small changes, referencing the geometry is unreliable if changes are anticipated. Rounds are similarly complex features whose resulting geometry can vary significantly with small changes.

Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Rule Check Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to custom RuleCHECK rules used within the model validation environment. RuleCHECK is commonly used to enforce company-specific modeling standards, design requirements, automation logic, and engineering practices that go beyond standard ModelCHECK functionality. This check helps expose rule-related information for review, reporting, and validation to ensure that custom rules are being applied consistently and functioning as intended. Improperly managed or undocumented rules can lead to inconsistent model quality, automation failures, and downstream manufacturing or data management issues. Rule definitions are typically configured through RuleCHECK configuration files and integrated ModelCHECK settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the RULECHECK_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Sharp Edges
Check.Description: Checks for edges or geometry transitions that create excessively sharp angles within the model. Sharp edges can introduce manufacturing difficulties, stress concentrations, handling hazards, assembly issues, and downstream problems in machining, molding, casting, or simulation processes. In many designs, sharp edges are replaced with rounds or chamfers to improve manufacturability, durability, and safety. This check helps identify geometry that may require smoothing or redesign to comply with company modeling and manufacturing standards. Sharp edge thresholds and related geometry limits are typically configured in the constant.mcn file using SHARP_ANGLE and related settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SHARP_EDGES settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Short Edges
Check.Description: Checks for very small or short edges within the model geometry that may create manufacturing, meshing, regeneration, or geometry quality problems. Short edges are often caused by poor modeling practices, failed geometry operations, tiny surface slivers, imported geometry issues, or excessive feature complexity. These edges can lead to unstable geometry, failed downstream operations, machining difficulties, simulation inaccuracies, and export or translation problems with formats such as STEP or IGES. This check helps identify geometry that may need simplification or cleanup to improve model robustness and manufacturability. Short edge size limits are typically configured in the constant.mcn file using SHORT_EDGE and SHORT_EDGE_BATCH settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SHORT_EDGES settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N E Y
Check.why:
You must be sensitive to accuracy settings when you have small geometry relative to the overall size of the model. If the accuracy is not set appropriately, geom checks could make the geometry unreliable in downstream work, as well as cause regeneration issues.
When a model has small geometry, the equivalent absolute accuracy should be two to three times smaller than the shortest edge. Some features have edges that are short in length. The definition of "short" varies depending on the overall size of the model and the current accuracy settings. If there are edges that are very short given the current accuracy settings, they are identified by this report.
### Short edges can occur in many situations. Some common cases include: - Transition geometry for round features. - External models that were merged or cut out of the current model. - External geometry referenced using copy geom and external copy geom features. - Imported geometry

Check.Name: Sheet Metal Bend Tables
Check.Description: Checks whether sheet metal models use valid and approved bend tables required for manufacturing calculations and flat pattern generation. Bend tables define how material behaves during bending operations and are critical for accurate bend allowances, bend deductions, flat lengths, and formed dimensions. Missing, incorrect, or nonstandard bend tables can result in inaccurate flat patterns, manufacturing errors, scrap, and problems during fabrication or CNC programming. This check helps ensure that sheet metal parts use the correct bend table definitions according to company manufacturing standards. Approved bend table names and related standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using SHTMTL_BTNAME entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SHTMTL_BENDTAB settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N E Y
Check.why: Missing sheet metal bend tables in sheet metal parts can lead to significant issues, including inaccurate bend allowances, incorrect part dimensions, and manufacturing errors, all of which compromise the part's functionality and increase the risk of costly rework and delays in production.

Check.Name: Sheet Metal Flat Patterns
Check.Description: Checks whether sheet metal parts contain valid flat pattern representations required for manufacturing, laser cutting, punching, and downstream fabrication processes. Flat patterns are critical for generating accurate unfolded geometry, material layouts, bend calculations, and production documentation. Missing, failed, or improperly defined flat patterns can lead to manufacturing errors, incorrect cut lengths, fabrication delays, and problems with CNC or nesting operations. This check helps ensure that sheet metal models are properly prepared for downstream manufacturing and documentation workflows. Sheet metal standards and related configuration settings are typically managed through sheet metal setup definitions and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SHTMTL_FLAT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N E Y
Check.why: The absence of sheet metal flat patterns in models can lead to significant problems, such as inaccuracies in the manufacturing process, difficulties in verifying part dimensions, and challenges in ensuring proper fit and function, ultimately compromising the efficiency and accuracy of production.

Check.Name: Sheet Metal Thickness
Check.Description: Checks whether sheet metal parts use valid and approved material thickness values according to company and manufacturing standards. Accurate sheet metal thickness is critical for bend calculations, flat pattern generation, tooling selection, structural performance, and downstream manufacturing processes such as laser cutting, forming, and CNC programming. Incorrect or unsupported thickness values can lead to fabrication errors, inaccurate flat patterns, tooling conflicts, and manufacturing delays. This check helps ensure that sheet metal parts use standardized and manufacturable thickness definitions. Approved sheet metal thickness values are typically configured in the start.mcs file using STD_SHTMTL_THICK_FILE entries and related external thickness tables, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SHTMTL_THICK settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Incorrect use of sheet metal thickness can lead to significant problems, such as structural weaknesses, fabrication errors, and improper fit, which can compromise model accuracy and hinder downstream processes like manufacturing and inspection. Ensuring all sheet metal thicknesses are correctly defined and within specified limits is essential for maintaining the integrity and functionality of the design.

Check.Name: Sheet Metal Unbend Features
Check.Description: Checks for unbend and bend-back features used within sheet metal parts. Unbend features are commonly used to temporarily flatten bends so additional geometry such as cuts, holes, or forms can be created in the flat condition before rebending the part. Improper use, excessive use, or failed unbend features can create unstable models, incorrect flat patterns, regeneration issues, and downstream manufacturing problems. This check helps ensure that unbend operations are used correctly and consistently to maintain accurate sheet metal behavior and manufacturable geometry. Sheet metal standards and related configuration settings are managed through ModelCHECK sheet metal configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SHTMTL_UNBENDS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N E Y
Check.why:
Missing unbend features in sheet metal parts can lead to significant issues, such as inaccuracies in flat pattern development, difficulties in verifying part dimensions, and challenges during manufacturing, all of which compromise the overall quality and functionality of the final product.

Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Sheet Metal Y Factor
Check.Description: Checks whether sheet metal parts use valid and approved Y-factor values for bend calculations and flat pattern development. The Y-factor defines how material stretches and compresses during bending and directly affects bend allowance, flat lengths, and formed dimensions. Incorrect or inconsistent Y-factor values can result in inaccurate flat patterns, poor fitment, manufacturing errors, scrap, and tooling issues during fabrication. This check helps ensure that sheet metal parts use standardized bend calculation behavior that aligns with company manufacturing practices and material specifications. Approved Y-factor values and related sheet metal standards are typically configured in external sheet metal configuration files and ModelCHECK setup files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SHTMTL_YFACTOR settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y
Check.why: Incorrect Y-factor values in sheet metal parts can lead to inaccuracies in bend allowances, resulting in incorrect part dimensions and potential manufacturing errors. Ensuring the Y-factor is correctly defined is crucial for maintaining the precision and functionality of the final product.

Check.Name: Simplified Representation Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to simplified representations used within the model or assembly. Simplified representations are commonly used to improve performance, reduce memory usage, simplify large assemblies, and control which components or features are displayed for different design, review, manufacturing, or simulation purposes. Improperly managed simplified representations can lead to missing geometry, incomplete assemblies, incorrect downstream exports, and confusion during collaboration or manufacturing processes. This check helps ensure that simplified representations are properly defined, named, and maintained according to company standards. Simplified representation naming and configuration rules are typically managed in the start.mcs file using PRT_SIMPREP_NAME and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SIMPREP_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y
Check.why:
Incorrectly defined or unused simplified representations in a model can cause misunderstandings, inaccuracies, and inefficiencies during the design process. Properly managing simplified representations helps organize work in large assemblies, reduces excess regeneration, and ensures clarity, accuracy, and efficiency in the design and review stages.

Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Simplified Representation Naming
Check.Description: Checks whether simplified representations follow approved company naming conventions and naming patterns. Consistent naming of simplified representations improves organization, readability, searchability, and communication when working with large assemblies, manufacturing views, simulation setups, and downstream processes. Poorly named or inconsistent simplified representations can create confusion, lead to incorrect representation usage, and make assembly management more difficult. This check helps ensure that simplified representations are clearly named and standardized across the organization. Naming rules for simplified representations are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_SIMPREP_NAME and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SIMPREP_NAME settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Sketch Items
Check.Description: Checks for sketches that contain excessive numbers of sketch entities or overly complex sketch geometry. Large or complicated sketches can make models difficult to understand, slow regeneration performance, increase rebuild instability, and create downstream manufacturing or editing problems. Overly complex sketches are often harder to troubleshoot, modify, and maintain, especially in large or highly parametric models. This check helps encourage simpler, cleaner sketching practices that improve model robustness, readability, and long-term maintainability. Sketch complexity limits are typically configured in the constant.mcn file using MAX_SKETCH_ITEMS and related settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SKETCH_ITEMS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why:
A sketches geometry is calculated automatically by powerful algorithms that reason about the entities, constraints and dimensions in a sketch. Without Sketcher, the resulting geometry is difficult to determine. You must still perform some level of reasoning about a sketch’s design intent as you create the sketched entities and dimensioning scheme. However, you do not have to perform the actual calculations and you can use Sketcher’s proposed dimensioning scheme to guide you and verify the sketch is fully constrained and dimensioned. Since you still need to reason and verify the design intent, you should strive to make the sketches as simple as possible without compromising the design.
### Simplifying Sketches: One way to simplify sketches is to separate the design intent into different features. For example, rounds and draft features are often better than adding their detail into a sketch. Another way to simplify sketch geometry is to create the constraints before you create dimensions. Adding a constraint often reduces the total number of constraints and dimensions proposed by Sketcher’s intent manager.
Imported sketch geometry can lead to many sketched entities. Even if you have to import the geometry, you may not need to reconstruct its design intent in Sketcher. If you do need to capture the design intent, you can separate the section’s geometry across multiple features so it is easier to reason about and verify. If you do have to capture design intent with Sketcher, then you can import the geometry as a datum curve. Another common problem with imported sketch geometry is that it may have many small entities that could be merged and/or simplified with a single spline entity. Pre-processing geometry to reduce the number of entities can be helpful before importing it (this is common with imported logo geometry).

Check.Name: SLA Features Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to SLA or STL-based geometry and features used for stereolithography, additive manufacturing, or rapid prototyping workflows. SLA-related geometry may include tessellated models, exported print geometry, or additive manufacturing preparation data used in downstream 3D printing processes. Improperly defined or poor-quality SLA geometry can lead to print failures, inaccurate surfaces, mesh problems, manufacturing defects, and downstream translation issues. This check helps identify and review SLA-related model information to improve additive manufacturing readiness and geometry quality. SLA-related standards and configuration settings are managed within the ModelCHECK configuration environment, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SLA_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Small Cylindrical Surfaces
Check.Description: Checks for very small cylindrical surfaces within the model geometry that may create manufacturing, meshing, regeneration, or geometry quality problems. Small cylindrical surfaces are often caused by tiny holes, sliver geometry, imported model defects, excessive feature detail, or poor modeling practices. These surfaces can lead to unstable geometry, machining difficulties, failed downstream operations, inaccurate simulations, and export or translation issues in formats such as STEP or IGES. This check helps identify geometry that may require simplification, cleanup, or redesign to improve model robustness and manufacturability. Cylindrical surface size limits are typically configured in the constant.mcn file using SMALL_RADIUS and related settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SMALL_CYLSRF settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N W Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Surface Edges
Check.Description: Checks for problematic surface edge conditions such as gaps, overlaps, sliver edges, or poorly connected surface boundaries within the model geometry. Poor surface edge quality can create unstable geometry, regeneration failures, downstream manufacturing problems, inaccurate simulations, and translation issues when exporting to formats such as STEP or IGES. Surface edge problems are especially important in complex surfacing, imported geometry, and Class-A surface design workflows where continuity and boundary quality are critical. This check helps identify geometry that may require repair, cleanup, or refinement to improve model integrity and downstream usability. Surface edge quality thresholds and related geometry settings are typically configured in the ModelCHECK geometry and constant configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SRF_EDGES settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Start Part Checks
Check.Description: Checks whether the model contains the required standard setup items defined in the company start model configuration. Start checks commonly verify required datums, parameters, layers, units, views, relations, materials, and other standard modeling elements that establish consistent design practices across parts, assemblies, and drawings. Missing or incorrect start-model content can weaken design intent, reduce standardization, create downstream manufacturing or documentation issues, and make models more difficult to maintain. This check helps ensure that models are built from approved standards and contain the foundational elements required by the organization. Start-model requirements are typically configured in the start.mcs file using entries such as PRT_PARAMETER, PRT_LAYER, PRT_DATUM_PLANE, MATERIAL_NAME, and related definitions, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the STARTCHECK settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N W Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Start Part Layer Check
Check.Description: Checks whether required standard layers defined by the company start model are present and correctly configured within the model. Standard layers are commonly used to organize datums, axes, curves, surfaces, annotations, construction geometry, and other model content according to consistent company practices. Missing or improperly configured layers can reduce model organization, create display and visibility issues, complicate downstream workflows, and make models more difficult to maintain and understand. This check helps ensure that models follow approved layer structure standards established by the organization. Required layer definitions and layer behavior are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_LAYER, ASM_LAYER, DRW_LAYER, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the STARTLAYR settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W E E Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Suppressed Features
Check.Description: Checks for features that have been suppressed within the model and may no longer actively contribute to the final geometry or design intent. Suppressed features are often used temporarily during design changes, troubleshooting, or configuration work, but unnecessary suppressed features can increase model complexity, create confusion, weaken maintainability, and introduce unexpected behavior during regeneration or downstream processes. Excessive suppressed features may also indicate unfinished design work or obsolete geometry that should be removed. This check helps identify suppressed features so models remain clean, stable, and easier to manage over time. Check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SUP_FEATURES settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N W Y
Check.why:
Leaving features suppressed in models can lead to significant problems later, as it can cause confusion, disrupt the model's intended functionality, create errors during regeneration, and complicate future modifications by masking dependencies and design intent.
Suppressed features are appropriate if they are only used by particular instances of the model, or for a simplified representation. Suppressed features in a master representation without a family table, however, indicate a problematic design intent that was suppressed temporarily. If the suppressed features are no longer needed, then they should be removed before you release the model. The presence of suppressed features adds confusion to your models, a well as runs the risk of having the features unintentionally resumed (or unsuppressed) some time in the future.

Check.Name: User-Defined Features
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to User-Defined Features (UDFs) used within the model. UDFs are reusable groups of standardized features commonly used to enforce consistent design practices, automate repetitive modeling tasks, and improve efficiency across parts and assemblies. Improperly defined, outdated, or inconsistently applied UDFs can lead to weak design intent, regeneration problems, inconsistent geometry, and downstream manufacturing or documentation issues. This check helps verify that UDFs are properly defined and used according to company modeling standards. UDF-related standards and configuration settings are managed within the ModelCHECK configuration environment, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the UDF_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Model Units for Length
Check.Description: Checks whether the model uses the correct length units required by company standards, such as millimeters, inches, or centimeters. Consistent unit systems are critical for maintaining accurate dimensions, mass properties, tolerances, manufacturing data, and interoperability with assemblies, drawings, simulations, CAM systems, and downstream data management tools. Incorrect length units can lead to scaling problems, manufacturing errors, assembly mismatches, and translation issues during export or collaboration. This check helps ensure that all models are created and maintained using approved unit standards. Required unit definitions are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_UNITS_LENGTH and ASM_UNITS_LENGTH entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the UNITS_LENGTH settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Unit Mass
Check.Description: Checks whether the model uses the correct mass units required by company standards, such as kilograms, pounds, or grams. Consistent mass units are critical for accurate mass properties, simulation results, manufacturing calculations, BOM data, and downstream integration with analysis, ERP, and data management systems. Incorrect mass units can lead to inaccurate weight calculations, assembly balance issues, simulation errors, and manufacturing or shipping problems. This check helps ensure that all models use approved and consistent mass unit definitions throughout the design process. Required mass unit standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_UNITS_MASS and ASM_UNITS_MASS entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the UNITS_MASS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y

Check.Name: View Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to saved views within the model, such as orientation views, standard views, and user-defined display views used for modeling, detailing, manufacturing, and downstream processes. Properly managed views improve model readability, communication, drawing creation, assembly navigation, and consistency across teams and workflows. Missing, inconsistent, or poorly organized views can create confusion, reduce efficiency, and lead to errors during detailing, manufacturing, inspection, or collaboration. This check helps ensure that required views are properly defined and maintained according to company standards. Standard view requirements are typically configured in the start.mcs file using PRT_VIEW, ASM_VIEW, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the VIEW_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Cross-Section Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to cross-sections created within the model. Cross-sections are commonly used to inspect internal geometry, communicate design intent, support detailing and manufacturing workflows, and analyze complex features or assemblies. Properly defined cross-sections improve model understanding, drawing clarity, inspection processes, and downstream manufacturing communication. Missing, inconsistent, or poorly managed cross-sections can reduce model usability and make internal geometry more difficult to review or validate. This check helps ensure that cross-sections are properly defined and maintained according to company standards. Cross-section requirements and related standards are typically configured within the ModelCHECK configuration environment and start.mcs file settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the XSEC_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: External Dependencies
Check.Description: Checks for references or dependencies between the current model and external models, assemblies, sketches, geometry, or copied data located outside the model itself. External dependencies can be useful for top-down design workflows, but unmanaged or unnecessary external references can weaken model stability, increase complexity, create regeneration failures, and make models difficult to modify, reuse, or transfer between projects. Broken or unintended external dependencies may also cause downstream issues in assemblies, manufacturing, Windchill, and data management processes. This check helps identify external references so they can be reviewed and managed according to company modeling practices. External reference behavior and related standards are typically controlled through Creo configuration settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the EXTERNAL_DEPS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why:
External dependencies in a model can lead to significant problems, such as increased complexity, reduced model stability, and challenges in maintaining and updating designs. Properly managing external dependencies is crucial for ensuring the integrity and reliability of the model, as well as facilitating efficient collaboration and modification processes.

Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Dependent Features
Check.Description: Checks for features that are highly dependent on other features through parent-child relationships within the model. While feature dependencies are a normal part of parametric modeling, excessive or poorly managed dependencies can weaken design intent, increase regeneration instability, and make models more difficult to modify, troubleshoot, and maintain. Changes to heavily referenced parent features may unintentionally affect large portions of the model and create cascading failures or unexpected geometry changes. This check helps identify complex dependency structures so models remain stable, maintainable, and easier to update over time. Feature dependency behavior and related modeling standards are managed through model design practices and ModelCHECK configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DEPENDENT_FEATURE settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Too many dependent features in a model can lead to significant problems, as changes to one feature can inadvertently affect others, complicating design modifications, increasing the risk of errors, and making the model more difficult to manage and maintain.

Check.Name: Designated Attributes
Check.Description: Checks whether required model parameters or attributes have been properly designated for use in downstream systems such as Windchill, BOM management, ERP integration, drawings, and manufacturing documentation. Designated attributes are commonly used to expose important metadata such as part numbers, descriptions, materials, lifecycle states, and custom business information outside the CAD model. Missing or incorrectly designated attributes can lead to incomplete data transfer, inconsistent documentation, automation failures, and downstream process errors. This check helps ensure that important model data is properly identified and available for enterprise and manufacturing workflows according to company standards. Designated attribute requirements are typically configured through Creo and Windchill attribute mapping settings along with ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DESIGNATED_ATTR settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Missing Standard Notes
Check.Description: Checks whether required standard notes are present within the model or drawing according to company drafting and documentation standards. Standard notes are commonly used to communicate critical manufacturing instructions, material requirements, tolerances, inspection information, safety notices, and other standardized engineering data. Missing standard notes can lead to incomplete documentation, manufacturing errors, inspection issues, and inconsistent communication across downstream processes. This check helps ensure that all required notes are included and properly defined to maintain drawing quality and compliance with organizational standards. Standard note requirements are typically configured in the start.mcs file using standard note definitions and related drawing configuration entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the STD_NOTES_MISSING settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Boundary Information
Check.Description: Checks Creo drawings to ensure that no views, entities, dimensions, symbols, notes, or tables are located outside the drawing sheet boundary. It is commonly used to identify misplaced drawing content that could affect drawing readability, plotting, or release quality.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N E Y

Check.Name: Overwritten Dimensions
Check.Description: Checks for dimensions in drawings that have been manually overwritten or replaced with custom text instead of displaying the true model-driven dimension value. Overwritten dimensions can hide the actual geometry definition, create inconsistencies between the drawing and the model, and introduce serious manufacturing, inspection, and quality risks. Because overwritten values may no longer update automatically when the model changes, they can lead to incorrect documentation, machining errors, rejected parts, and downstream communication problems. This check helps ensure that dimensions remain associative, accurate, and fully driven by the underlying model geometry according to company drafting standards. Dimension display behavior and related detailing standards are managed through Creo drawing settings and ModelCHECK drafting configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DIM_OVERWRITE settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N E Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Draft Geometry
Check.Description: Checks for draft or manually created drawing geometry that is not directly associated with model geometry. Draft geometry may include manually sketched lines, notes, symbols, or annotation entities added directly within the drawing for clarification or cosmetic purposes. While draft geometry can be useful for supplemental documentation, excessive or improperly managed draft entities can reduce drawing associativity, create inconsistencies between the drawing and the model, and increase the risk of outdated or incorrect documentation during design changes. This check helps identify manually created drawing geometry so it can be reviewed and managed according to company drafting and documentation standards. Draft geometry standards and related drawing practices are typically configured through Creo detailing settings and ModelCHECK drawing configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DRAFT_GEOM settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N E Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Drawing Layers Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to layers used within drawings, including layer names, visibility states, organization, and assignment of drawing entities such as dimensions, annotations, symbols, notes, views, and reference geometry. Properly managed drawing layers improve drawing readability, visibility control, drafting consistency, manufacturing communication, and downstream inspection workflows. Poorly organized or inconsistent drawing layers can create visual clutter, hide important information, complicate revision management, and reduce overall drawing quality. This check helps ensure that drawing layers are properly defined and maintained according to company drafting standards. Drawing layer definitions and standards are typically configured in the start.mcs file using DRW_LAYER, DRW_LAYER_MOVE, DRW_LAYER_UNWANTED, and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DRAWING_LAYERS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N E Y

Check.Name: Drawing Parameters
Check.Description: Checks whether required drawing parameters are present, correctly defined, properly formatted, and compliant with company drafting and documentation standards. Drawing parameters are commonly used to store and display critical metadata such as drawing numbers, descriptions, revisions, materials, project information, approval status, and manufacturing data. Missing, incorrect, or inconsistent drawing parameters can lead to incomplete title blocks, incorrect BOM data, failed Windchill or ERP integration, and downstream manufacturing or inspection issues. This check helps ensure that drawing metadata is complete, accurate, and consistently applied throughout the documentation process. Drawing parameter requirements and validation rules are typically configured in the start.mcs file using DRW_PARAMETER and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DRAWING_PARAMS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N E Y

Check.Name: Drawing Naming
Check.Description: Checks whether drawing names comply with company-defined naming standards and approved naming patterns. Consistent drawing naming improves organization, traceability, searchability, revision control, and communication across design, manufacturing, inspection, Windchill, and downstream data management systems. Incorrect or inconsistent drawing names can lead to duplicate documentation, revision confusion, incorrect model associations, and downstream manufacturing or ERP errors. This check helps ensure that drawings are clearly and consistently named according to organizational standards and documentation practices. Drawing naming rules are typically configured in the start.mcs file using DRW_MODEL_NAME and related drawing naming entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DRAWING_NAME settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N W Y

Check.Name: Default Views
Check.Description: Checks whether drawings contain the required default views defined by company drafting standards and approved drawing templates. Default views commonly include standard orientations such as front, top, right, isometric, section, or auxiliary views that are necessary for clearly communicating part geometry, dimensions, manufacturing requirements, and inspection information. Missing or improperly configured default views can reduce drawing clarity, create interpretation errors, and lead to downstream manufacturing or assembly issues. This check helps ensure that drawings contain the expected standard views and maintain consistent documentation practices across the organization. Default drawing view requirements are typically configured through drawing templates, Creo detailing settings, and ModelCHECK drafting configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the DEFAULT_VIEWS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N W Y

Check.Name: Surfaces without GDT Annotations
Check.Description: Checks for model surfaces that are not referenced by dimensions, GDT annotations, notes, surface finish symbols, inspection annotations, or other manufacturing and documentation annotations. Unannotated surfaces may indicate incomplete model-based definition (MBD) practices, missing manufacturing requirements, undocumented inspection criteria, or incomplete engineering communication. Surfaces lacking proper annotation can create ambiguity during manufacturing, quality inspection, assembly, and downstream interpretation of the design intent. This check helps ensure that important surfaces are properly documented and associated with the required annotation information according to company MBD and drafting standards. Annotation requirements and related documentation practices are typically configured through Creo annotation settings and ModelCHECK drafting configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SRF_NOT_REF_BY_ANNTN settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N W Y

Check.Name: Annotations missing in Combination State
Check.Description: Checks whether required annotations are included and properly displayed within defined combination states used for Model-Based Definition (MBD) workflows. Combination states control the visibility of annotations, views, geometry, and display settings used to communicate manufacturing, inspection, assembly, and engineering requirements directly within the 3D model. Missing annotations in combination states can result in incomplete documentation, incorrect manufacturing interpretation, inspection errors, failed design reviews, and downstream communication problems. This check helps ensure that all required dimensions, GDT annotations, notes, and manufacturing information are visible in the appropriate combination states according to company MBD standards. Combination state and annotation requirements are typically configured through Creo MBD settings and ModelCHECK drafting configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the ANNTN_NOT_ON_COMB_STATE settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N W Y

Check.Name: Annotations not Referencing any Surface
Check.Description: Checks for annotations that are not associated with or attached to specific model surfaces or geometry references. Annotations without valid surface references may indicate incomplete Model-Based Definition (MBD) practices, broken annotation associations, improperly placed dimensions, or unclear manufacturing and inspection requirements. Unreferenced annotations can create ambiguity during design review, manufacturing, quality inspection, and downstream interpretation of the model, increasing the risk of errors and inconsistent documentation. This check helps ensure that annotations remain properly connected to the intended geometry and accurately communicate design intent according to company MBD and drafting standards. Annotation association requirements and related documentation practices are typically configured through Creo annotation settings and ModelCHECK drafting configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the ANNTN_MISSING_SURF_REF settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N W Y

Check.Name: Erased Views
Check.Description: Checks for drawing views that have been erased, hidden, or removed from active display within the drawing. Erased views may still exist in the drawing database even though they are no longer visible, and unmanaged erased views can increase drawing complexity, create confusion, affect performance, and complicate drawing maintenance or revision control. In some cases, erased views may indicate obsolete documentation, incomplete cleanup, or outdated design information that should be reviewed or permanently removed. This check helps ensure that drawing views are properly managed and that unnecessary or hidden views do not negatively affect drawing quality and maintainability. Drawing view management practices and related standards are typically configured through Creo detailing settings and ModelCHECK drafting configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the ERASED_VIEWS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N W Y

Check.Name: Format Naming
Check.Description: Checks whether drawing formats and format file names comply with approved company naming standards and documentation practices. Drawing formats define standard borders, title blocks, zones, notes, and layout information used across engineering drawings, and consistent format naming helps ensure proper document control, template usage, revision management, and downstream manufacturing communication. Incorrect or inconsistent format names can lead to use of outdated templates, incomplete title block information, drafting inconsistencies, and downstream documentation errors. This check helps ensure that drawings use the correct approved formats and naming conventions according to organizational standards. Format naming rules and approved format definitions are typically configured in the start.mcs file using DRW_FORMAT_NAME and related drawing configuration entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the FORMAT_NAME settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Ignored Sheets
Check.Description: Checks for drawing sheets that have been marked to be ignored or excluded from certain ModelCHECK evaluations, printing workflows, or documentation processes. Ignored sheets may be intentionally excluded for legacy content, reference information, temporary work, or specialized documentation purposes, but improperly managed ignored sheets can create confusion, incomplete documentation, revision inconsistencies, and downstream manufacturing or inspection issues. Hidden or excluded sheets may also contain outdated or uncontrolled information that bypasses normal quality checks. This check helps identify ignored sheets so they can be reviewed and managed according to company drafting and documentation standards. Drawing sheet management practices and related standards are typically configured through Creo detailing settings and ModelCHECK drawing configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the IGNORE_SHEETS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Models Used in Drawing
Check.Description: Checks and reports information about the models, parts, assemblies, and references used within the drawing. Drawings often reference multiple models for views, sections, details, BOMs, family tables, and assembly documentation, and properly managing these model references is critical for maintaining drawing accuracy, associativity, revision control, and downstream manufacturing communication. Missing, outdated, incorrect, or unnecessary model references can create drawing inconsistencies, broken views, BOM errors, and downstream data management or manufacturing problems. This check helps ensure that drawings reference the correct approved models and maintain proper model-to-drawing relationships according to company standards. Drawing model reference rules and related standards are typically configured through Creo drawing settings and ModelCHECK drafting configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the MODELS_USED settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y Y N Y

Check.Name: Note Case Errors
Check.Description: Checks whether notes in the drawing use the correct text case formatting according to company drafting standards, such as uppercase, lowercase, or mixed-case requirements. Consistent note formatting improves drawing readability, professionalism, standardization, and communication across engineering, manufacturing, inspection, and downstream documentation processes. Incorrect note case can create inconsistent documentation, reduce clarity, and violate company or industry drafting practices. This check helps ensure that all drawing notes follow approved formatting and presentation standards. Note formatting rules and related drafting standards are typically configured through Creo detailing settings and ModelCHECK drawing configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the NOTE_CASE settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N W Y

Check.Name: Non-standard Note Fonts
Check.Description: Checks whether notes in the drawing use the approved font types defined by company drafting and documentation standards. Consistent note fonts improve drawing readability, professionalism, print quality, and standardization across engineering, manufacturing, inspection, and downstream documentation workflows. Incorrect or inconsistent fonts can reduce clarity, create formatting issues during printing or PDF generation, and lead to noncompliant drawing presentation standards. This check helps ensure that all drawing notes use approved font settings and maintain consistent visual formatting throughout the documentation package. Note font requirements and related drafting standards are typically configured through Creo detailing settings and ModelCHECK drawing configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the NOTE_FONT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Non-standard Note Heights
Check.Description: Checks whether notes in the drawing use the correct text height according to company drafting and documentation standards. Consistent note height improves drawing readability, print quality, professionalism, and communication across engineering, manufacturing, inspection, and downstream documentation workflows. Notes that are too small may become unreadable, while oversized notes can create cluttered or unbalanced drawings. Incorrect note heights may also violate company or industry drafting standards and reduce overall drawing quality. This check helps ensure that all drawing notes use approved text sizing and maintain consistent visual presentation throughout the drawing package. Note height requirements and related drafting standards are typically configured through Creo detailing settings and ModelCHECK drawing configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the NOTE_HEIGHT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Note Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to notes used within the drawing, including note content, formatting, associations, placement, and compliance with company drafting standards. Notes are commonly used to communicate manufacturing instructions, inspection requirements, material specifications, process information, safety notices, and other critical engineering documentation. Missing, incorrect, poorly formatted, or inconsistent notes can create confusion, manufacturing errors, inspection issues, and downstream communication problems. This check helps ensure that drawing notes are properly defined, organized, and maintained according to approved documentation practices. Note formatting rules, required note content, and related drafting standards are typically configured through Creo detailing settings and ModelCHECK drawing configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the NOTE_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Note Spelling Errors
Check.Description: Checks notes within the drawing for spelling errors, invalid words, or text that does not comply with company-approved terminology and documentation standards. Correct spelling is important for maintaining drawing clarity, professionalism, manufacturing accuracy, inspection communication, and downstream documentation quality. Misspelled notes can create confusion, misinterpretation of design intent, manufacturing mistakes, and inconsistencies across engineering documentation. This check helps ensure that drawing notes use clear, accurate, and standardized language according to company drafting practices. Spelling validation rules, approved terminology, and related drafting standards are typically configured through Creo detailing settings, custom dictionaries, and ModelCHECK drawing configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the NOTE_SPELL settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N E Y

Check.Name: Title Block Spelling Errors
Check.Description: Checks titles within the drawing for spelling errors, invalid words, or text that does not comply with company-approved terminology and documentation standards. Drawing titles are critical for identification, traceability, revision control, manufacturing communication, and downstream data management processes, and misspelled or inconsistent titles can create confusion, searchability issues, incorrect documentation, and ERP or Windchill integration problems. This check helps ensure that drawing titles use accurate, professional, and standardized language according to company drafting practices. Spelling validation rules, approved terminology, and related drafting standards are typically configured through Creo detailing settings, custom dictionaries, and ModelCHECK drawing configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the TITLE_SPELL settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N E Y

Check.Name: Symbol Spelling
Check.Description: Checks symbols within the drawing for spelling errors, invalid words, or text that does not comply with company-approved terminology and documentation standards. Symbols often contain important manufacturing, inspection, welding, GDT, and process-related information, and misspelled symbol text can create confusion, interpretation errors, and downstream manufacturing or quality issues. Consistent and correctly spelled symbol content improves drawing clarity, professionalism, and communication across engineering and production workflows. This check helps ensure that symbol text uses accurate and standardized language according to company drafting practices. Spelling validation rules, approved terminology, and related drafting standards are typically configured through Creo detailing settings, custom dictionaries, and ModelCHECK drawing configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SYMBOL_SPELL settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Unacceptable Notes
Check.Description: Checks for notes in the drawing that contain prohibited, obsolete, nonstandard, or otherwise unacceptable text according to company drafting and documentation standards. Unacceptable notes may include outdated instructions, unauthorized wording, incorrect terminology, duplicate information, uncontrolled abbreviations, or text that conflicts with approved engineering and manufacturing practices. Invalid notes can create confusion, manufacturing errors, inspection issues, compliance problems, and downstream documentation inconsistencies. This check helps ensure that only approved note content and standardized language are used throughout the drawing package. Unacceptable note definitions and approved terminology are typically configured in the start.mcs file using DRW_NOTE_UNACCEPT and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the NOTE_UNACCEPT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Drawing Sheets
Check.Description: Checks whether the drawing contains an acceptable number of sheets according to company documentation and complexity standards. Excessive numbers of drawing sheets can make documentation difficult to manage, review, print, revise, and maintain, while too few sheets may result in overcrowded views, poor readability, and incomplete communication of manufacturing or inspection information. Monitoring sheet count helps maintain clear, organized, and efficient drawing packages that support downstream manufacturing, quality, and data management processes. This check helps ensure that drawing sheet usage remains practical and compliant with organizational drafting standards. Drawing sheet count limits and related documentation standards are typically configured through ModelCHECK drawing configuration settings and company drafting practices, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the NUM_DRAW_SHEETS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Overlapping Information
Check.Description: Checks for overlapping drawing information such as dimensions, notes, symbols, annotations, tables, views, balloons, or other entities that interfere with each other visually within the drawing. Overlapping information can reduce readability, create confusion, hide important manufacturing or inspection details, and increase the risk of interpretation errors during production and quality processes. Poorly organized drawing layouts may also violate company drafting standards and reduce overall documentation quality. This check helps identify crowded or conflicting drawing content so layouts can be cleaned up and presented more clearly. Drawing spacing rules and related detailing standards are typically configured through Creo detailing settings and ModelCHECK drawing configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the OVERLAP_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N E Y

Check.Name: Drawing Parameters Existence
Check.Description: Checks whether required parameters exist within the drawing and are properly defined according to company drafting and documentation standards. Drawing parameters are commonly used to store and display important metadata such as drawing numbers, descriptions, revisions, materials, approvals, project information, and manufacturing data. Missing or improperly defined parameters can lead to incomplete title blocks, incorrect BOM data, failed Windchill or ERP integration, and downstream manufacturing or inspection issues. This check helps ensure that all required drawing metadata is present, accurate, and consistently maintained throughout the documentation process. Required drawing parameter definitions are typically configured in the start.mcs file using DRW_PARAMETER and related entries, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the PARAMS_EXIST settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Unused Drawing Parameters
Check.Description: Checks whether a drawing is using the required standard parameters that are driven from model parameters. The expected parameters are defined in the start configuration file using the DRW_PARAMETER option, and ModelCHECK reports a failure if those required parameters are not referenced in the drawing (for example, in title blocks, notes, or tables). This check helps ensure that drawing information remains linked to model data and is not manually entered or missing.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Regeneration of Dimensions
Check.Description: Checks whether drawing dimensions regenerate and update correctly after model changes, drawing updates, or regeneration operations. Dimensions that fail to regenerate properly may become disconnected, display incorrect values, lose associativity with model geometry, or create outdated manufacturing and inspection information within the drawing. Regeneration problems can lead to inaccurate documentation, revision inconsistencies, manufacturing errors, and downstream quality issues. This check helps ensure that dimensions remain fully associative, stable, and synchronized with the underlying model geometry throughout the design process. Dimension regeneration behavior and related detailing standards are typically configured through Creo drawing settings and ModelCHECK drafting configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the REGEN_DIMS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N E Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Sheet Size
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to drawing sheet sizes used within the drawing package. Sheet size standards are important for maintaining consistent documentation layouts, print scaling, title block formatting, readability, plotting compatibility, and downstream manufacturing or archival processes. Incorrect or nonstandard sheet sizes can create printing issues, formatting inconsistencies, revision control problems, and difficulties with document management systems. This check helps ensure that drawings use approved sheet sizes and comply with company drafting and documentation standards. Approved sheet size definitions and related drawing format standards are typically configured through drawing templates, Creo detailing settings, and ModelCHECK drafting configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SHEET_SIZE_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Standard Detail Setup
Check.Description: Checks whether the drawing uses the correct standard detail setup according to company drafting templates and documentation rules. The standard detail setup defines the baseline configuration for drawing behavior, including dimensioning standards, text styles, view settings, annotation rules, and general drafting environment settings. If the detail setup is incorrect or inconsistent, drawings may display inconsistent formatting, incorrect dimension behavior, nonstandard annotations, and downstream issues in manufacturing, inspection, and data management processes. This check helps ensure that drawings conform to the approved company-standard detailing environment for consistent and high-quality documentation. Standard detail setup definitions are typically configured in the start.mcs file using detail setup files (DTL) and related Creo configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the STD_DTL_SETUP settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Symbol Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to symbols used within the drawing, including their definitions, usage, placement, and compliance with company drafting standards. Drawing symbols are commonly used to communicate manufacturing requirements, welding information, surface finish, GDT elements, and other standardized annotations critical to design intent and downstream processes. Incorrect, missing, or inconsistently used symbols can lead to misinterpretation of manufacturing instructions, inspection errors, and reduced drawing clarity. This check helps ensure that all symbols are properly defined, consistently used, and aligned with company documentation standards. Symbol definitions and usage rules are typically configured through Creo symbol files and ModelCHECK drafting configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SYMBOL_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Table Cell Information
Check.Description: Checks the contents, structure, and configuration of table cells within a drawing to ensure they comply with company drafting and documentation standards. Table cells are commonly used in BOMs, revision tables, notes, parameter-driven tables, and other structured documentation elements, where consistency in formatting, content, and layout is critical for readability and downstream use. Incorrect, inconsistent, or improperly configured table cells can lead to misinterpretation of data, formatting issues, failed automation, and downstream manufacturing or data management errors. This check helps ensure that all table cells are correctly defined, consistently formatted, and properly populated according to organizational standards. Table cell rules and formatting requirements are typically configured through Creo table definitions and ModelCHECK drafting configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the TABLE_CELLS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Title Information
Check.Description: Checks and reports information related to titles used within the drawing, including title content, formatting, placement, and compliance with company drafting and documentation standards. Drawing titles are critical for identifying the part or assembly, supporting revision control, enabling searchability, and ensuring correct association with manufacturing, inspection, and downstream systems such as Windchill or ERP. Missing, incorrect, or inconsistently formatted titles can lead to confusion, misidentification of drawings, and downstream documentation errors. This check helps ensure that all drawing titles are properly defined, consistently formatted, and aligned with organizational standards. Title requirements and formatting rules are typically configured through Creo drawing setup files and ModelCHECK drafting configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the TITLE_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Unused Models
Check.Description: Checks whether the drawing contains referenced models (parts or assemblies) that are not actually used in any drawing views, tables, annotations, or other downstream drawing elements. Unused models can increase drawing complexity, cause confusion about design intent, slow performance, and introduce maintenance risks when models are updated or replaced. They may also indicate leftover references from earlier design iterations or incomplete cleanup of drawing content. This check helps ensure that only relevant and actively used models are included in the drawing, improving clarity, efficiency, and data integrity. Model usage rules and reference management are typically controlled through Creo drawing setup and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the UNUSED_MODELS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N E Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Unused Sheets
Check.Description: Checks whether the drawing contains sheets that exist but are not actively used for any meaningful content such as views, tables, notes, annotations, or manufacturing information. Unused sheets can increase drawing complexity, create confusion during review or revision, and lead to inefficient document management. They may also indicate incomplete cleanup from earlier design iterations or leftover template content that no longer serves a purpose. This check helps ensure that drawings only contain necessary and actively used sheets, improving clarity, organization, and maintainability. Sheet usage rules and drawing structure standards are typically configured through Creo drawing setup and ModelCHECK drafting configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the UNUSED_SHEETS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y
