Check.Name: View Scale
Check.Description: Checks whether drawing views use appropriate and standardized scale values according to company drafting and documentation rules. Proper view scaling is essential for maintaining drawing readability, ensuring correct representation of geometry, and supporting downstream manufacturing, inspection, and interpretation processes. Incorrect or inconsistent view scales can lead to misinterpretation of dimensions, poor drawing clarity, and noncompliance with drafting standards. This check helps ensure that all drawing views use approved scale values that maintain consistency and clarity across the drawing package. View scale requirements and acceptable scale values are typically configured through Creo drawing setup files and ModelCHECK drafting configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the VIEW_SCALE settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N W Y

Check.Name: Bill of Material
Check.Description: Checks whether the assembly bill of materials (BOM) is correctly defined, structured, and synchronized with the assembly model. The BOM represents the hierarchical structure of components used in an assembly and is critical for manufacturing planning, procurement, cost estimation, configuration control, and downstream systems such as ERP and Windchill. Missing, incorrect, or inconsistent BOM data can lead to incorrect part counts, assembly errors, procurement mistakes, and manufacturing delays. This check helps ensure that the BOM accurately reflects the assembly structure and complies with company data management and documentation standards. BOM definitions and related configuration rules are typically controlled through Creo assembly settings, repeat region tables, and Windchill integration mappings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the ASM_BOM settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Assembly Features
Check.Description: Checks whether assembly-level features are properly defined, structured, and managed within the model according to company modeling standards. Assembly features include cuts, holes, merges, patterns, and other operations created at the assembly level that affect multiple components or control assembly-specific geometry. Improper or excessive use of assembly features can weaken model clarity, increase regeneration complexity, create unexpected behavior across components, and make assemblies harder to maintain or modify. This check helps ensure that assembly features are used appropriately and that design intent remains clear, stable, and consistent throughout the assembly structure. Assembly feature usage and related modeling standards are typically configured through Creo assembly settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the ASM_FEATURES settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Bulk Items
Check.Description: Checks for bulk items used within an assembly and verifies that they are properly defined and managed according to company modeling and manufacturing standards. Bulk items typically represent non-modeled or non-solid components such as grease, oil, adhesive, sealant, weld material, or other consumable or distributed materials that are not represented as discrete parts in the BOM. Improper or missing bulk item definitions can lead to inaccurate BOMs, incorrect cost calculations, missing manufacturing requirements, and downstream errors in procurement or production planning. This check helps ensure that all required bulk items are correctly included and consistently defined within the assembly structure. Bulk item definitions and related configuration rules are typically managed through Creo assembly settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the BULK_ITEMS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Frozen or Suspended Components
Check.Description: Checks for components that have been frozen within an assembly and verifies that their usage aligns with company modeling and design control practices. Frozen components are typically locked in place to prevent updates or regeneration, which can help stabilize critical portions of an assembly during development. However, excessive or inappropriate use of frozen components can mask design issues, prevent necessary updates, weaken model associativity, and lead to inconsistencies between intended and actual design state. This check helps identify frozen components so they can be reviewed to ensure they are intentionally and appropriately used within the assembly. Component freeze behavior and related assembly control practices are typically managed through Creo assembly settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the FRZ_COMPONENTS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y
Check.why: Freezing a component is useful when debugging retrieval or regeneration issues. However, it is only a temporary solution. If an assembly’s design is to be released, the frozen components should be unfrozen. Components are frozen when the component is missing or could not be retrieved or regenerated successfully. In this case, you can use the component in its last known, successfully regenerated state in order to make progress with your design. Before releasing the model, you should unfreeze the component and resolve the reference issue, otherwise you risk releasing with an out of date component.

Check.Name: Generic Components Use
Check.Description: Checks whether generic family table components are used within an assembly and verifies that only appropriate instance models are placed in the design. Generic models are intended to serve as master templates for family table instances and are typically not meant to be assembled directly. Using generics in assemblies can lead to incorrect BOM data, inaccurate mass properties, invalid dimensions, and downstream manufacturing or documentation errors because the generic does not represent a specific producible configuration. This check helps ensure that assemblies use only valid family table instances, maintaining correct configuration control, design intent, and data integrity. Family table usage rules and component selection standards are typically configured through Creo family table settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the GEN_COMPONENTS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Possible Global Interferences
Check.Description: Checks whether an assembly has had a Global Interference analysis performed within the company-defined time period. When a Global Interference analysis is run in Creo, ModelCHECK records the date by creating or updating a parameter in the assembly. During subsequent ModelCHECK runs, the GLOBAL_INTF check compares the age of this parameter against the configured maximum number of days (GLOBAL_INTF_DAYS). If the analysis is older than the allowed period or has never been performed, the check reports the assembly according to the severity defined in the GLOBAL_INTF setting of the check.mch configuration file. This check does not perform a Global Interference analysis itself; it only verifies that one has been completed recently enough to meet company standards, helping ensure that component interferences are periodically reviewed throughout the design process.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y

Check.Name: Missing Components
Check.Description: Checks whether all required components are present in the assembly and identifies any missing parts that should be included according to the design definition, BOM structure, or referenced assembly configuration. Missing components can result from broken references, incomplete assembly regeneration, suppressed or excluded parts, configuration errors, or improper data management. These issues can lead to inaccurate BOMs, incomplete assemblies, manufacturing errors, incorrect mass properties, and downstream documentation or validation failures. This check helps ensure that assemblies are complete, consistent, and correctly represent the intended product structure. Component inclusion rules and assembly structure validation are typically managed through Creo assembly settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the MIS_COMPONENTS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y

Check.Name: Parts and Sub-assemblies
Check.Description: Checks whether the number of components in an assembly is within the limits defined by company standards and expected design complexity guidelines. Excessive component counts can negatively impact assembly performance, regeneration time, system memory usage, manageability, and downstream processes such as BOM handling, manufacturing planning, and simulation. Too few components may also indicate an incomplete or under-defined assembly structure. This check helps ensure that assemblies remain within an acceptable complexity range appropriate for their intended use and performance requirements. Component quantity limits and related assembly complexity standards are typically configured through ModelCHECK performance and configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the NUM_COMPONENTS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y Y Y Y

Check.Name: Suppressed Components
Check.Description: Checks for components in the assembly that are currently suppressed and verifies whether their suppression status is appropriate according to company modeling and design practices. Suppressed components are temporarily excluded from the active assembly structure and do not participate in regeneration, BOM generation, mass properties, or downstream processes. While suppression can be useful for design iteration and configuration control, excessive or improperly managed suppressed components can hide incomplete design work, introduce confusion, and lead to inconsistencies in assembly structure, documentation, and manufacturing outputs. This check helps ensure that suppressed components are intentional, controlled, and properly managed within the assembly lifecycle. Component suppression rules and assembly configuration practices are typically managed through Creo assembly settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SUP_COMPONENTS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E W W Y
Check.why:
Suppressed components and features are displayed in the model tree but they are not regenerated. If a feature is suppressed, it is essentially turned off.
Suppressed components and features are appropriate if they are only used by particular instances of the model, or for a simplified representation.
Suppressed features in a master representation without a family table, however, indicate a problematic design intent that was suppressed temporarily.
If the suppressed features are no longer needed, then they should be removed before you release the model. The presence of suppressed features adds confusion to your models, a well as runs the risk of having the features unintentionally resumed (or unsuppressed) some time in the future.

Check.Name: Packaged Components
Check.Description: Checks for components in the assembly that are in a packaged state and verifies that they are properly managed according to company modeling and assembly practices. Packaged components are typically placed in an assembly without full constraint definition, allowing them to be positioned loosely during early design stages or layout development. While this can be useful for flexibility during conceptual design, improperly managed packaged components can lead to unstable assemblies, unclear design intent, regeneration inconsistencies, and downstream issues in BOM accuracy, manufacturing, and data management processes. This check helps ensure that packaged components are intentional, properly controlled, and transitioned to fully constrained states when required. Component packaging rules and assembly constraint practices are typically managed through Creo assembly settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the PACK_COMPONENTS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E W W Y
Check.why: Errors with packaged components in an assembly can lead to instability and functionality issues during design changes. Packaged components, which are parts temporarily placed in an assembly without being fully constrained, are valuable because they allow for flexibility during the design process. Ensuring these components are correctly identified and managed is crucial for maintaining assembly stability and functionality, facilitating easier modifications and adjustments.

Check.Name: Unique Parts and Sub-assemblies
Check.Description: Checks for components in the assembly that are marked as unique instances and verifies that they are properly defined and managed according to company modeling and configuration control standards. Unique components are non-reusable, instance-specific parts that differ from other occurrences of the same model and are typically used when geometry, parameters, or configurations vary from standard instances. While unique components are sometimes necessary for special design cases, excessive or uncontrolled use can reduce reuse efficiency, increase data management complexity, complicate BOM structure, and introduce inconsistencies in manufacturing, costing, and downstream systems. This check helps ensure that unique components are used intentionally, properly documented, and aligned with company design and configuration management practices. Unique component rules and assembly management practices are typically configured through Creo assembly settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the UNQ_COMPONENTS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y Y Y Y

Check.Name: Circular References
Check.Description: Checks for circular references within an assembly where components or features depend on each other in a closed loop relationship. Circular references can occur when parent-child relationships, external references, or parametric dependencies are improperly defined, causing regeneration instability, unpredictable model behavior, or complete regeneration failures. These issues can significantly compromise assembly stability, design intent, and downstream processes such as manufacturing, simulation, and data management. This check helps identify and break circular dependency chains to ensure a robust, stable, and maintainable assembly structure. Circular reference rules and dependency management practices are typically controlled through Creo modeling standards and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the CIRCULAR_REFS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N N Y
Check.why:
Circular references occur when a chain of references form a loop. The models in the circular loop cannot be fully regenerated.
Circular references typically occur across different components in an assembly. Individually, the components may appear to retrieve normally and new features can sometimes be added without problems, but attempts to regenerate the complete model fail. Circular references should be removed when they occur.
Best practice methods and standards around reference control can help eliminate the possibility of creating circular references.

Check.Name: Weak Sketcher Dimensions
Check.Description: Checks for weak sketcher dimensions in parts and assemblies, where dimensions are not fully driving the sketch geometry or are automatically generated as non-driving references by Creo. Weak dimensions indicate that sketch geometry is not fully controlled by explicit constraints and design intent, making the model more vulnerable to unintended changes during edits, regeneration, or downstream use. In both part and assembly sketches, reliance on weak dimensions can lead to unstable geometry, inconsistent updates, and reduced model robustness. This check helps identify sketches that contain weak or non-driving dimensions so they can be converted into fully defined, intentional, and robust constraint systems. Sketch constraint standards are typically governed by Creo sketcher settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the WEAK_SKETCHER_DIMS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E E E Y
Check.why: ### Why Avoid Weak Sketcher Dimensions? Weak sketcher dimensions, also known as non-driving dimensions, are automatically generated by Creo to define the geometry of a sketch. While they can be convenient during the initial stages of design, relying on them can lead to significant issues later in the design and manufacturing process.

Check.Name: Unattached Feature
Check.Description: Checks for features in the model that are not properly attached or referenced within the design structure, meaning they are not fully integrated into the parent-child feature tree or do not have valid geometric or parametric dependencies. Unattached features can occur due to incomplete feature creation, broken references, failed regeneration, or deleted parent geometry. These features may behave unpredictably during regeneration, contribute to model instability, and lead to errors in downstream processes such as manufacturing, simulation, and drawing creation. This check helps identify such orphaned or disconnected features so they can be repaired, reattached, or removed to maintain a stable and fully defined model. Feature attachment rules and model integrity standards are typically governed by Creo modeling practices and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the UNATTACHED_FEAT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W E N Y

Check.Name: Incomplete Features Defined
Check.Description: Checks for features in the model that are incomplete or not fully defined according to Creo feature definition requirements. Incomplete features may result from interrupted feature creation, missing inputs, failed references, unresolved geometry, or partially defined parameters. These features can cause regeneration failures, unstable model behavior, incorrect geometry output, and downstream issues in drawings, manufacturing, simulation, and data exchange. This check helps identify incomplete features so they can be repaired or completed to ensure a fully defined and stable model. Feature completion and model integrity rules are typically governed by Creo modeling standards and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the AF_INCOMPLETE settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W E N Y

Check.Name: Assembly Missing References
Check.Description: Checks for missing or unresolved references within an assembly that are required for proper model regeneration and correct assembly behavior. Missing references can occur when parent components, geometry, datums, constraints, or external dependencies are deleted, renamed, suppressed, or fail to load correctly. These issues can break assembly constraints, cause regeneration failures, result in misplaced components, and lead to incorrect geometry, BOM data, and downstream manufacturing or documentation errors. This check helps identify broken or incomplete reference chains so they can be repaired or redefined to restore full assembly stability and design intent. Assembly reference integrity rules are typically governed by Creo assembly constraint and dependency management settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the AE_MISSINGREFS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W E N Y
Check.why: Features can reference design information in the current model or an external model. When this design information is no longer present, your model cannot be properly retrieved or regenerated. Common causes of missing references are missing models that must be located, or a design change that eliminated the original reference. The best way to fix a missing reference is to redefine the feature or component placement constraints. To temporarily fix a missing reference you can suppress the feature, or suppress or freeze the component. While suppressed or frozen, work can continue until the missing references are recovered. This is a risky practice; when the feature or component is resumed or unfrozen, the geometry could be in a different state that makes the following dependent work invalid.

Check.Name: Small Curves
Check.Description: Checks for very small or tiny curve elements in the model that fall below acceptable geometric thresholds defined by company standards or VDA/quality guidelines. These tiny curve segments can indicate poor-quality geometry, imported data issues, over-detailed modeling, or unintended artifacts from operations such as trimming, blending, or surface intersection. Excessively small curve elements can negatively impact model performance, regeneration stability, downstream CAM operations, and data exchange robustness. This check helps identify and flag such small curve entities so the model can be cleaned, simplified, or repaired to improve geometric quality and manufacturability. Curve quality thresholds and VDA-related geometry limits are typically configured through ModelCHECK geometry and quality settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the M1_TINY_ELMNT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Identical Element Curves
Check.Description: Checks for duplicate or identical curve elements within the model and reports instances where multiple geometric entities are effectively the same. Identical element curves can arise from redundant modeling operations, repeated imports, copy/paste geometry creation, or overlapping design intent that was not properly consolidated. These redundancies can increase model complexity, degrade performance, complicate downstream CAM or analysis workflows, and introduce confusion during design review or modification. This check helps identify duplicate curve geometry so the model can be cleaned, simplified, and optimized for better performance and maintainability. Geometry redundancy rules and VDA-related quality thresholds are typically configured through ModelCHECK geometry and quality settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the M2_IDENTICAL_ELMNT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Positional Discontinuity Curves (Gaps)
Check.Description: Checks for positional continuity across connected surfaces or curves within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will verify that surfaces or curves are seamlessly connected at their boundaries without gaps or misalignments, ensuring a smooth and continuous transition between them. This helps maintain the geometric integrity and quality of the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Positional Discontinuity Topologies
Check.Description: Checks for positional continuity across topological boundaries within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will verify that surfaces or curves are properly aligned at their topological boundaries, ensuring that there are no gaps or misalignments. This helps maintain the geometric integrity and ensures a smooth connection between adjacent surfaces or features.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Curves Tangential Angle Discontinuity
Check.Description: Checks for tangential continuity across connected surfaces or curves within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will verify that the tangency between surfaces or curves is smooth and continuous at their boundaries, ensuring there are no abrupt changes in direction or angle, which is essential for maintaining the quality and smoothness of the model's geometry.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Tangential Angle Discontinuity Topologies
Check.Description: Checks for tangential continuity across topological boundaries within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will ensure that connected surfaces or curves maintain smooth tangency across their topological boundaries, preventing abrupt changes in direction or angle. This helps maintain the surface quality and smoothness in critical areas of the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Curves with Curvature Discontinuity
Check.Description: Number of curves with curvature discontinuity greater than some delta. Checks for curvature continuity across connected surfaces or curves within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will verify that the curvature transitions smoothly across boundaries without abrupt changes, ensuring that the connected surfaces or curves maintain a consistent curvature, which is important for the aesthetic and functional quality of the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Surface Boundaries with Curvature Discontinuity
Check.Description: Checks for curvature continuity across topological boundaries within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will verify that the curvature is smoothly continuous across these boundaries, ensuring that there are no abrupt changes in curvature, which is crucial for maintaining high-quality surface transitions and overall model integrity.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Curves with Polynomial Degree
Check.Description: Checks the degree of polynomial used in defining curves or surfaces within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will verify that the polynomial degree is within the predefined acceptable range and report any deviations, ensuring that the curves or surfaces are appropriately defined for the intended design, analysis, or manufacturing processes.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Wavy Curves
Check.Description: Checks for waviness in surfaces or curves within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will evaluate the smoothness of surfaces or curves, identifying and reporting any irregularities or deviations that cause waviness, ensuring that the model's surfaces are smooth and meet design quality standards.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Curves with Small Distance between Knot Vectors
Check.Description: Checks the distance between knots in a spline or curve within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will verify that the spacing between knots meets predefined criteria and report any instances where the knot distance is too small, ensuring that the curve is smooth and free from issues that could affect the model's geometry or downstream processes.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Curves that Self Intersect
Check.Description: Checks the distance of a feature or element from itself to ensure it meets predefined minimum criteria. When enabled, ModelCHECK will identify and report any features that are too close to themselves, such as self-intersecting surfaces or curves, helping to prevent geometric issues that could complicate manufacturing or analysis.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Drawing Text (Chars) that aren't IGES Compliant
Check.Description: Checks for text elements in a model that may not conform to IGES (Initial Graphics Exchange Specification) standards. When enabled, ModelCHECK will verify that the text in the model is compatible with IGES export requirements and report any issues, ensuring that the model can be correctly translated into IGES format without losing or misrepresenting text information.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Face Segments with Small Edges
Check.Description: Checks for tiny segment edges within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will identify and report edges that are excessively short, below a predefined minimum length, helping to ensure that the model does not contain small, potentially problematic edges that could cause issues in manufacturing, analysis, or overall model quality.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Surfaces with Curvature less than Some Number
Check.Description: Checks for curvature radii that are too small within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will identify and report instances where the curvature radius is below a predefined minimum threshold, helping to ensure that the model does not contain extremely tight curves that could cause issues in manufacturing, analysis, or functionality.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Surfaces where the Angle between Adjacent Surfaces is Outside Range
Check.Description: Checks the angle between edge curves at their junctions within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will verify that the angles between boundary curves meet predefined criteria and report any angles that are too sharp or do not conform to the design standards, helping to ensure smooth transitions and proper geometric relationships in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Surfaces with Reversed Normals
Check.Description: Checks for reversals in surface normals within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will identify and report instances where surface normals are oriented in opposite or incorrect directions, helping to ensure that the surfaces are consistently oriented and that the model is free from issues that could affect manufacturing, analysis, or rendering.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Surfaces with Greater than X Patches
Check.Description: Checks the distribution of patches in a surface mesh to ensure they are evenly spaced. When enabled, ModelCHECK will verify that the distance between patches within a surface meets predefined criteria and report any inconsistencies, helping to ensure that the surface mesh is uniform and correctly defined.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Surfaces with Unoccupied Patch Rows
Check.Description: Checks for unoccupied patch rows in a surface mesh within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will identify and report any rows in the surface patch grid that lack patches or are not fully occupied, ensuring that the surface mesh is complete and properly defined. This helps maintain the integrity and accuracy of the surface geometry in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Surfaces that have Boundary Curves that Extend Greater than X from the Extremity of the Surface
Check.Description: Checks the proximity of a boundary curve to its associated surface within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will verify that the boundary curve is correctly positioned relative to the surface and report any deviations that exceed predefined criteria. This ensures that the boundary curves are accurately aligned with the surfaces, maintaining the intended design integrity.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: All Surfaces have Similar Orientation
Check.Description: Checks for similar orientation of curves or paths within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will verify that paths or curves have a consistent orientation relative to each other and report any inconsistencies, ensuring that the model's geometry follows the intended design directions and is free from orientation issues that could impact functionality or aesthetics.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Faces with More than X Segments per Face
Check.Description: Checks the number of segments in a boundary curve within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will verify that the number of segments in the curve meets predefined criteria and report any instances where the segmentation is either too high or too low, helping to ensure smooth and accurate boundary definitions in the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Edges with at Least 3 Faces
Check.Description: Checks the number of faces connected to an edge within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will verify that the number of faces sharing an edge meets the predefined criteria and report any instances where this number is unusual or problematic. This helps ensure the model's geometry is correct and avoids potential issues in manufacturing or structural analysis.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Normals Improperly Oriented
Check.Description: Checks the orientation of surface normals to ensure they are consistent and correctly aligned within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will verify that similar surfaces have their normals oriented in the same direction and report any inconsistencies. This helps prevent issues related to surface orientation that could affect manufacturing, analysis, or the overall quality of the model.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Knife Edges
Check.Description: Identifies and reports the presence of knife edges in a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will check for edges that are extremely sharp, resembling a knife edge, which could lead to manufacturing or structural issues. This check helps ensure that the model does not contain any problematic sharp edges that could compromise the design's integrity or manufacturability.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Vertices Not in Line with Edges
Check.Description: Checks the distance between vertices and adjacent edges within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will verify that the distance between vertices and their neighboring edges meets the predefined criteria, and it will report any instances where the distance is too small or not as expected. This ensures proper geometric relationships within the model, helping to avoid potential issues in manufacturing or analysis.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Vertices Not in Line with Faces
Check.Description: Checks the distance between vertices and adjacent faces within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will verify that the distance between vertices and their neighboring faces meets the predefined criteria, and it will report any instances where the distance is too small or not as expected. This helps ensure proper geometric relationships within the model, avoiding potential issues in manufacturing or analysis.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Imported Features
Check.Description: Identifies and reports any deletion of history within a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will check for instances where feature history has been removed or altered, ensuring that the model's historical data is intact and that any deletions are intentional and compliant with design practices. This helps maintain the integrity of the model's construction history, which is crucial for understanding and modifying the design later on.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Extra Geometrical Features
Check.Description: Identifies and reports the presence of extra or unnecessary geometry in a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will check for and report any additional geometry that is not required for the intended design, helping to ensure that the model remains clean, efficient, and free from redundant elements.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Unintentional Cavities
Check.Description: Identifies and reports the presence of multiple internal cavities or voids (buried features) within a solid body. When enabled, ModelCHECK will check for these cavities and report them, ensuring that they are consistent with the design intent and do not pose any issues in manufacturing or analysis.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y
Check.why: A buried feature has geometry that is entirely surrounded (or enclosed) by another feature’s geometry. For example, a cut feature may be completely filled by one or more features. Or a solid feature, such as a protrusion, may be completely surrounded by geometry from one or more other features. The design intent of a buried feature should be questioned since it is being replaced by geometry from other features. Often buried features indicate a model that was modified when undesired geometry was cut away or an undesired region was filled in to start over. This may feel like a pragmatic solution, but it is only a short term solution. When the undesired design intent is left behind (or buried by geometry from new features), it makes the model unnecessarily complex. Good design intent should be understandable and the designer should make changes by modifying and redesigning the existing design intent. Leaving features that do not contribute to the design intent is confusing for new designers.

Check.Name: Number of Multi-Body (Merged) Solids
Check.Description: Checks for part models that contain multiple solid bodies (merged or non-merged) and reports their presence according to company modeling standards. Multi-body parts can occur when geometry is combined through merges, imports, Boolean operations, or when separate solid regions are intentionally maintained within a single part file. While multi-body modeling can be valid in certain advanced design or manufacturing scenarios, uncontrolled or unintended use can increase model complexity, create ambiguity in design intent, complicate mass properties, interfere with manufacturing workflows, and introduce issues in downstream processes such as CAM programming, simulation, and data management. This check helps ensure that the use of multiple solid bodies is intentional, properly controlled, and aligned with company design and modeling guidelines. Multi-body modeling rules and part structure standards are typically configured through Creo part modeling practices and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SO26_MULT_BODY settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N N Y

Check.Name: Number of Multi-Solid Parts
Check.Description: Checks for part models that contain multiple solid bodies and reports instances where more than one independent solid exists within a single part file. Multi-solid parts can result from Boolean operations, merges, imports, or intentional design strategies where separate solids are maintained within one model. While this approach can be valid in specific cases, uncontrolled or unintended multi-solid modeling can increase model complexity, create ambiguity in design intent, complicate mass property calculations, and introduce difficulties in downstream processes such as manufacturing planning, CAM programming, simulation, and data management. This check helps ensure that part models conform to company standards that may require a single solid body per part unless explicitly justified. Multi-solid modeling rules and part structure requirements are typically configured through Creo part modeling practices and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SO27_MULT_SOLID settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N N Y

Check.Name: Duplicate annotation elements
Check.Description: Checks for duplicate geometric tolerance (GTOL) annotation elements within the model or drawing and identifies cases where multiple instances of the same tolerance definition exist. Duplicate GTOL annotations can occur due to copy/paste operations, repeated annotation creation, inconsistent detailing practices, or incomplete cleanup of legacy annotation data. These redundancies can create confusion in manufacturing and inspection interpretation, introduce conflicting requirements, and reduce overall clarity and quality of the model-based definition or drawing. This check helps ensure that geometric tolerances are unique, properly managed, and consistently applied according to company drafting and MBD standards. GTOL annotation rules and quality requirements are typically configured through Creo annotation settings and ModelCHECK drafting configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the AE_GTOL_DUPLICATE settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Duplicate surface-finish annotation elements
Check.Description: Checks for duplicate surface finish annotation elements within a model or drawing and identifies cases where multiple instances of the same surface finish requirement exist. Duplicate surface finish annotations can occur due to repeated detailing, copy/paste errors, inconsistent annotation practices, or incomplete cleanup of legacy data. These redundancies can create confusion in manufacturing interpretation, lead to conflicting surface quality requirements, and reduce clarity in model-based definition or drawing documentation. This check helps ensure that surface finish requirements are uniquely defined, properly managed, and consistently applied according to company drafting and MBD standards. Surface finish annotation rules and quality requirements are typically configured through Creo annotation settings and ModelCHECK drafting configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the AE_SF_DUPLICATE settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Inactive annotations
Check.Description: Checks for annotations in the model or drawing that are currently inactive, suppressed, or not visible in the active display state. Inactive annotations may still exist in the model database but are not contributing to the current documentation, which can lead to incomplete communication of design intent, missed manufacturing requirements, or confusion during review and downstream processes. These conditions often arise from combination states, display configurations, legacy annotation practices, or incomplete cleanup of outdated annotation data. This check helps ensure that all required annotations are active, visible where intended, and properly contributing to the model-based definition or drawing. Annotation activation and display rules are typically managed through Creo annotation settings and ModelCHECK MBD/drafting configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the ANNTN_INACTIVE settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Failed Components
Check.Description: Checks for assembly components in a failed state due to missing references, invalid geometry, broken constraints, unresolved features, or other modeling failures. Failed components can compromise overall assembly integrity, lead to incorrect geometry, prevent successful regeneration, and cause cascading errors throughout dependent components, drawings, BOMs, and downstream manufacturing or simulation processes. These issues often arise from incomplete design changes, deleted references, external dependency failures, or incompatible model updates. This check helps ensure that all components in the assembly are fully functional, successfully regenerated, and consistent with the intended design state. Component regeneration rules and failure reporting are typically governed by Creo assembly diagnostics and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the FAILED_COMPONENTS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N Y Y

Check.Name: Fixed Components
Check.Description: Checks for components in an assembly that are fixed (fully constrained in position) and verifies that their fixed status is intentional, appropriate, and consistent with company modeling and assembly design practices. Fixed components do not move relative to the assembly and are often used as reference anchors or to stabilize key parts of a design. However, excessive or improper use of fixed components can reduce design flexibility, hide constraint issues, weaken assembly intent, and lead to inaccurate behavior during redesign, motion studies, or configuration changes. This check helps ensure that fixed components are used deliberately and correctly positioned within the assembly structure. Component fixation rules and assembly constraint standards are typically governed by Creo assembly practices and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the FIXED_COMPONENTS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Flexible Components
Check.Description: Checks for components in the assembly that are defined as flexible and verifies that their flexible behavior is correctly configured and functioning according to company modeling and design intent standards. Flexible components can change shape or configuration depending on their placement context within an assembly, allowing a single model to adapt to multiple conditions such as variable geometry, mechanisms, or configurable subassemblies. While flexibility can be powerful for design reuse and kinematic behavior, incorrect or unintended flexible definitions can lead to unpredictable geometry changes, constraint instability, regeneration issues, and inconsistencies in downstream manufacturing, simulation, and BOM reporting. This check helps ensure that flexible components are intentionally used and properly controlled so their behavior remains consistent with design requirements. Flexibility rules and assembly behavior settings are typically managed through Creo assembly configuration options and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the FLEX_COMPONENTS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Mechanism Components
Check.Description: Checks for mechanical components in an assembly and verifies that they are properly defined, configured, and compliant with predefined modeling and design standards. Mechanical components typically include parts involved in motion, load transfer, kinematic behavior, or mechanism simulation such as gears, linkages, springs, cams, fasteners, and motion-driving elements. Incorrect or inconsistent definition of mechanism components can lead to assembly errors, incorrect motion behavior, constraint failures, regeneration issues, and inaccurate simulation or analysis results. This check helps ensure that mechanical components are correctly identified, properly defined, and function as intended within the assembly structure. Mechanism definition rules and assembly behavior standards are typically managed through Creo mechanism design settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the MECH_COMPONENTS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Datum Planes
Check.Description: Checks whether the model contains the correct set of datum planes as defined by the company template requirements and verifies that they are properly named, structured, and present in the model. Datum planes form the foundational reference geometry for sketches, features, assemblies, and downstream design intent, and missing or incorrect datum planes can lead to weak model structure, inconsistent referencing, regeneration instability, and difficulties in maintaining design standards across parts and assemblies. This check helps ensure that all required datum planes exist and conform to the expected template-based structure so that the model maintains a consistent and stable reference framework. Datum plane standards and template requirements are typically defined in the start model or template configuration files and enforced through ModelCHECK configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the TEMPLATE_DATUM_PLANES settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Children of External Failed Components
Check.Description: Checks for external child components in an assembly that have failed due to issues with their external parent components and verifies their dependency status and integrity. These failures typically occur when external references, skeleton models, top-down design elements, or externally driven geometry are broken, missing, or no longer valid. When an external parent component fails, any dependent child components may also become unstable, fail to regenerate, or display incorrect geometry. This can lead to cascading assembly errors, broken design intent, inaccurate downstream manufacturing data, and unreliable simulation results. This check helps identify components impacted by external dependency failures so the root cause can be resolved and assembly integrity restored. External dependency rules and failure handling are typically managed through Creo external reference controls and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the CHILD_EXT_FAILED_COMPONENTS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N Y Y

Check.Name: Children of External Failed Features
Check.Description: Checks for external child features in a model that have failed due to issues with their external parent features and evaluates their dependency status within the feature structure. These failures typically arise in top-down design workflows where features reference external geometry, skeleton models, layouts, or other externally driven design elements. When an external parent feature becomes invalid, missing, or fails to regenerate, any dependent child features may also fail or produce incorrect geometry. This can result in broken design intent, regeneration errors, unstable model behavior, and downstream impacts on manufacturing, simulation, and documentation accuracy. This check helps identify external dependency failures at the feature level so they can be diagnosed and corrected to restore model integrity. External feature dependency rules and failure handling are typically managed through Creo external reference controls and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the CHILD_EXT_FAILED_FEATURES settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N Y Y

Check.Name: Children of Failed Components
Check.Description: Checks for child components in an assembly that have failed due to issues with their parent components and evaluates the impact of cascading failures within the assembly structure, where a parent component that fails to regenerate, loses references, or contains invalid geometry can cause dependent child components to also fail, become unstable, or generate incorrect positioning or geometry, leading to broken design intent, compromised assembly integrity, and downstream issues in manufacturing, simulation, BOM accuracy, and documentation, and this check helps identify affected child components so that the root cause in the parent component can be corrected and full assembly stability restored, while assembly dependency and failure propagation behavior are governed by Creo regeneration and reference handling mechanisms and ModelCHECK configuration settings controlled through the CHILD_FAILED_COMPONENTS check in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N Y Y

Check.Name: Children of Failed Features
Check.Description: Checks for child features in a model that have failed due to issues with their parent features and evaluates the propagation of those failures through the feature dependency chain within the model. When a parent feature becomes invalid, loses references, contains errors, or fails to regenerate, any dependent updatable child features may also fail or produce incorrect geometry, leading to cascading regeneration problems, broken design intent, and downstream issues in manufacturing, simulation, and documentation. This check helps identify affected child features so that the root cause in the parent feature can be corrected and overall model stability restored. Feature dependency behavior and failure propagation are governed by Creo modeling rules, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the CHILD_FAILED_FEATURES settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N Y Y

Check.Name: Drawing Symbols
Check.Description: Checks that all symbols used in the model or drawing conform to approved company or project standard symbol libraries and verifies that they are correctly applied and consistently defined across the documentation. Standard symbols are critical for communicating manufacturing, inspection, welding, GDT, and other engineering requirements, and the use of nonstandard or incorrectly applied symbols can lead to misinterpretation, production errors, and inconsistencies in downstream processes. This check helps ensure that only approved symbols are used and that they match the definitions and usage rules defined in the company’s symbol standards. Symbol validation rules and approved libraries are typically configured through Creo symbol file libraries and ModelCHECK configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the STD_SYMBOLS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Failed Features
Check.Description: Checks for features in the model that have failed to regenerate or execute properly due to missing references, invalid inputs, geometry errors, or broken dependencies, and evaluates their impact on overall model integrity and stability. Failed features can disrupt the feature regeneration sequence, create invalid or incomplete geometry, and cause cascading issues in dependent features, assemblies, drawings, and downstream processes such as manufacturing, simulation, and data exchange. This check helps identify all failed features so they can be diagnosed, corrected, or rebuilt to restore a fully functional and stable model. Feature regeneration rules and failure handling are governed by Creo modeling behavior and ModelCHECK configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the FAILED_FEATURES settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N Y Y

Check.Name: Flexible Modeling Features
Check.Description: Checks for the presence of flexible modeling features within a part or assembly and verifies that their use complies with company design standards and intended modeling practices. Flexible modeling features allow geometry to be locally modified without fully redefining upstream features, which can be useful for design iteration and quick modifications, but if used excessively or inappropriately they can weaken design intent, create unpredictable regeneration behavior, introduce hidden dependencies, and complicate downstream manufacturing, simulation, and data management processes. This check helps ensure that flexible modeling is used intentionally and controlled so that model stability and consistency are maintained. Flexible modeling rules and feature usage standards are typically governed by Creo modeling practices and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the FLEXIBLE_MODELING_FEATURES settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Feature without surfaces that may be buried
Check.Description: Checks for suspect buried features in the model that may have no exposed or accessible surfaces and are potentially hidden within surrounding geometry, which can indicate modeling errors, incomplete feature definition, or unintended internal geometry. Buried features can cause issues in manufacturing, assembly interpretation, interference detection, and downstream processes such as CAM programming and simulation because they may not be visible or easily accessible for validation or modification. This check helps identify features that may be fully or partially enclosed so they can be reviewed and corrected to ensure model integrity and manufacturability. Buried feature detection rules and geometry validation standards are typically governed by Creo modeling practices and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the BURIED_SUSPECT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y
Check.why:
A buried feature has geometry that is entirely surrounded (or enclosed) by another feature’s geometry. For example, a cut feature may be completely filled by one or more features. Or a solid feature, such as a protrusion, may be completely surrounded by geometry from one or more other features. The design intent of a buried feature should be questioned since it is being replaced by geometry from other features. Often buried features indicate a model that was modified when undesired geometry was cut away or an undesired region was filled in to start over. This may feel like a pragmatic solution, but it is only a short term solution. When the undesired design intent is left behind (or buried by geometry from new features), it makes the model unnecessarily complex. Good design intent should be understandable and the designer should make changes by modifying and redesigning the existing design intent. Leaving features that do not contribute to the design intent is confusing for new designers.

Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Small Distance Between Edges
Check.Description: Checks the distance between boundaries in a model to ensure they meet predefined criteria. When enabled, ModelCHECK will measure the proximity of boundaries and report any that are too close or too far apart, helping to ensure that the model's boundaries are correctly spaced according to design requirements.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Small Element for Faces
Check.Description: Identifies and reports tiny facets in a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will check for extremely small facet elements that could cause issues in rendering, analysis, or manufacturing, and report them, helping to ensure that the model's surface quality is maintained and free from problematic tiny facets.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Small Element for Surface
Check.Description: Identifies and reports tiny surface elements in a model. When enabled, ModelCHECK will check for extremely small surface elements that could cause issues in manufacturing or analysis and report them, helping to ensure that the model is free from problematic tiny surfaces that could affect its performance.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Interfering Bodies
Check.Description: Checks for interference between solid bodies within a multibody part and verifies whether any bodies intersect or occupy the same physical space in a way that violates design intent or structural integrity. Body interference can occur due to overlapping features, incorrect Boolean operations, imported geometry conflicts, or incomplete modeling steps, and it can lead to manufacturing issues, simulation errors, incorrect mass properties, and downstream assembly or validation failures. This check helps identify internal conflicts between solid bodies so they can be resolved to ensure a clean, manufacturable, and structurally valid multibody model. Multibody interference rules and geometry validation standards are typically governed by Creo part modeling practices and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the BODY_INTF settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Lightweight Holes
Check.Description: Checks for lightweight hole features in a model and verifies that they are correctly defined, fully resolved, and compliant with company design and modeling standards. Lightweight holes are typically simplified or performance-optimized representations used to reduce regeneration time and improve large model performance, but if they are incorrectly defined or missing full definition data they can lead to incomplete geometry, inaccurate manufacturing outputs, poor mass property calculations, and issues in downstream processes such as CAM, simulation, and drawing creation. This check helps ensure that lightweight hole representations are valid, consistent, and do not compromise model accuracy or performance. Lightweight feature handling rules and performance settings are typically governed by Creo modeling and performance configuration options and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the HOLE_LIGHTWT settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Material Card Densities
Check.Description: Checks whether the material density assigned to the model is correctly defined and compliant with company-approved material standards and material card definitions. Material density is critical for accurate mass properties, inertia calculations, simulations, structural analysis, and downstream manufacturing and costing processes, and incorrect or missing density values can lead to significant errors in weight calculations, analysis results, and product documentation. This check helps ensure that the assigned material density matches approved material cards and remains consistent with engineering specifications and company material libraries. Material density validation rules and approved material definitions are typically managed through Creo material libraries and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the MATERIAL_DENSITY settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Model Detail Setup
Check.Description: Checks whether the model is using the correct predefined standard detail setup and verifies compliance with company or project-specific detailing configurations. The detail setup controls core drafting behavior such as dimensioning rules, text styles, annotation display, tolerances, view display settings, and other model detailing parameters that directly affect drawing consistency and documentation quality. If the model does not match the required standard detail setup, it can result in inconsistent drafting behavior, nonstandard annotations, incorrect dimension display, and downstream issues in manufacturing, inspection, and data exchange. This check helps ensure that all models follow the approved detailing environment to maintain consistency, accuracy, and compliance with organizational standards. Detail setup definitions and configuration rules are typically managed through Creo detail setup files and ModelCHECK configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the MDL_STD_DTL_SETUP settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Model has Mutli-bodies
Check.Description: Checks whether the part model contains multiple solid bodies and reports cases where more than one body exists when a single-body design standard is expected according to company modeling guidelines. Multi-body parts can arise from Boolean operations, merges, imports, or intentional design strategies, but if not properly controlled they can introduce ambiguity in design intent, complicate mass property calculations, increase modeling complexity, and create issues in downstream processes such as manufacturing, CAM programming, simulation, and data management. This check helps ensure that multi-body usage is intentional, properly justified, and aligned with organizational standards for part structure. Multi-body modeling rules and part structure requirements are typically governed by Creo part modeling practices and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the MULTIBODY_MODEL settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW Y Y N Y Y

Check.Name: Duplicate Models
Check.Description: Checks the active model against existing models in the database by comparing geometry, topology, and key model properties to identify potential duplicates or near-duplicates. Duplicate models can lead to redundant data, confusion in design ownership, incorrect reuse of parts, and inefficiencies in data management systems such as Windchill or PLM databases. By identifying models that are geometrically or structurally similar, this check helps prevent unnecessary duplication, improves reuse of existing designs, and supports cleaner, more efficient model libraries. Duplicate detection rules and search behavior are typically configured through ModelCHECK database connectivity settings and company PLM integration standards, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SEARCH_DUP_MODELS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Simplified Rep Master
Check.Description: Checks whether the master representation of a simplified representation (SIMPRep) is correctly defined and consistently used within the model according to company standards. The SIMPRep master defines the full-detail version that governs all simplified configurations, and incorrect or inconsistent master definitions can lead to incorrect display states, missing geometry in simplified modes, inconsistent assembly behavior, and downstream errors in manufacturing, analysis, and documentation. This check helps ensure that the master representation is properly designated, structurally valid, and aligned with the intended simplification strategy for the model. SIMPRep standards and configuration rules are typically managed through Creo representation settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SIMPREP_MASTER settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Validate Dimensions
Check.Description: Checks whether model dimensions are accurate, valid, and compliant with predefined company drafting and design standards. This includes verifying that dimensions correctly reflect the underlying geometry, follow proper dimensioning practices, and adhere to required tolerances, formatting rules, and annotation conventions. Incorrect or nonstandard dimensions can lead to manufacturing errors, inspection issues, misinterpretation of design intent, and downstream problems in documentation and data exchange. This check helps ensure that all model dimensions are reliable, consistent, and aligned with engineering requirements. Dimension validation rules and standards are typically managed through Creo detailing settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the VALIDATE_DIMENSIONS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Validate Geometric Tolerance Values
Check.Description: Checks whether geometric tolerances (GTOL) in the model are correctly defined and comply with predefined company engineering and manufacturing standards. This includes validating that tolerance values are appropriate for the intended function, consistent with design requirements, and properly applied to the correct features without conflicts or over/under specification. Incorrect or inconsistent GTOL values can lead to manufacturing difficulty, inspection failures, part nonconformance, and downstream assembly or functional issues. This check helps ensure that all geometric tolerances are meaningful, correctly defined, and aligned with quality and design intent requirements. GTOL validation rules and tolerance standards are typically managed through Creo annotation settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the VALIDATE_GTOL_VALUES settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Legacy Datum Tag Annotation
Check.Description: Checks whether legacy set datum features and secondary datum target annotations (SDTAs) are correctly applied and compliant with predefined company standards for datum definition and annotation practices. This includes verifying that datum features and their associated targets are properly defined, consistently referenced, and conform to current modeling and drafting requirements, as legacy or incorrectly applied datum annotations can lead to ambiguity in inspection definitions, manufacturing errors, inconsistent GDT interpretation, and downstream quality issues. This check helps ensure that datum structures and secondary datum target annotations are valid, correctly implemented, and aligned with modern design intent and standards. Datum annotation rules and legacy compliance requirements are typically managed through Creo annotation settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the VALIDATE_SET_DATUM_AND_SDTA settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Map Parameters
Check.Description: Checks whether model parameters are correctly mapped from one predefined name to another according to company naming and standardization rules. This includes validating that renamed or legacy parameters are properly converted into the approved parameter names used for consistency across models, drawings, BOMs, and downstream systems such as Windchill or ERP. Incorrect or missing parameter mappings can lead to inconsistent metadata, broken automation, incorrect reporting, and data integration issues across engineering and manufacturing workflows. This check helps ensure that parameter naming conventions are standardized and consistently applied throughout the model. Parameter mapping rules and naming standards are typically managed through ModelCHECK configuration files and parameter definition settings in Creo, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the PARAM_MAP settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN N N N Y Y

Check.Name: Unwanted Parameters
Check.Description: Checks for parameters in the model that are not part of the approved company standard set or are explicitly marked as unwanted according to configuration rules. Unwanted parameters may include obsolete metadata, incorrectly created parameters, legacy naming conventions, or user-defined attributes that are not required for design, manufacturing, or downstream system integration such as BOM generation, Windchill, or ERP workflows. The presence of such parameters can lead to model clutter, confusion, inconsistent data management, and potential automation or reporting errors. This check helps ensure that only approved and necessary parameters exist in the model, supporting clean, standardized, and efficient data structures. Parameter control rules and approved/blocked parameter lists are typically managed through ModelCHECK configuration files and Creo parameter settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the PARAM_UNWANTED settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N Y Y

Check.Name: Parent Reference Issues
Check.Description: Checks for features in the model whose parent-child relationships differ from expected design intent or from the original feature structure defined during model creation. This includes identifying cases where features have been reordered, reattached, or unintentionally linked to different parent features, which can occur due to edits, regeneration changes, or geometry modifications. Incorrect parent relationships can compromise model stability, lead to unexpected regeneration behavior, break downstream features, and create inconsistencies in design intent and documentation. This check helps ensure that the feature hierarchy remains consistent, predictable, and aligned with the intended modeling structure. Parent-child relationship rules and model integrity standards are typically governed by Creo feature dependency behavior and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the PARENT_DIFF settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Missing Parameters
Check.Description: Checks whether required start parameters are present in the model and verifies that all mandatory parameters defined by company standards are correctly created at the beginning of the modeling process. Mandatory parameters are not stored within ModelCHECK itself but are defined in external start configuration sources such as the start.mcs file and Creo start model templates (for example start.prt, start.asm, and start.drw), where required parameters are specified using entries like PRT_PARAMETER, ASM_PARAMETER, and DRW_PARAMETER. This check compares the active model against these predefined start definitions to ensure that all required parameters exist and have not been removed or omitted, helping maintain consistent model metadata, proper configuration control, and reliable downstream use in drawings, BOMs, and enterprise systems such as Windchill or ERP. Parameter requirements and validation behavior are controlled by the STARTPARM settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y
Check.why: Help on this topic...

Check.Name: Non-regenerating Simplified Reps
Check.Description: Checks whether all simplified representations (SREPs) in the model can regenerate successfully and verifies that they are properly defined, valid, and functional within the model structure. Non-regenerating simplified representations can occur due to missing references, broken dependencies, geometry changes, or invalid configuration definitions, and they can lead to incorrect display states, incomplete model data, and downstream issues in assembly performance, documentation, and manufacturing outputs. This check helps ensure that all simplified representations are stable, up to date, and correctly regenerating as part of the model’s configuration management. Simplified representation regeneration behavior and validation rules are controlled by the REGEN_SREPS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N Y Y
Check.why: Cross-sections are imaginary cuts through parts and assemblies that aid with visualizing and understanding the geometry inside a model. Each cross-section is associatively linked to the model’s geometry and updates when a change occurs.

Check.Name: Non-regenerating Cross Sections
Check.Description: Checks whether all cross-sections in the model can regenerate successfully and verifies that they are properly defined, associative, and functional within the model structure. Non-regenerating cross-sections can occur due to broken references, geometry changes, missing parent features, or invalid section definitions, and can lead to incorrect section views, incomplete visualization of internal geometry, and downstream issues in design review, manufacturing communication, and documentation accuracy. This check helps ensure that all cross-sections remain valid and update correctly when the model changes, maintaining reliable visualization and analysis of internal geometry. Cross-section regeneration behavior and validation rules are controlled by the REGEN_XSEC settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N Y Y
Check.why: Cross-sections are imaginary cuts through parts and assemblies that aid with visualizing and understanding the geometry inside a model. Each cross-section is associatively linked to the model’s geometry and updates when a change occurs.

Check.Name: Relation Update
Check.Description: Checks for relations in the model that are outdated, incorrectly evaluated, or require regeneration to reflect the current state of the model geometry and parameters. Relations define parametric equations and dependencies between dimensions, features, and parameters, and if they are not updated correctly they can produce incorrect geometry, inconsistent design intent, regeneration failures, and downstream issues in drawings, manufacturing, and simulation. This check helps ensure that all relations are current, properly evaluated, and consistent with the active model state. Relation update behavior and validation rules are controlled by the RELATION_UPDATE settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Unwanted Relations
Check.Description: Checks for relations in the model that are not permitted or are considered unwanted according to predefined company criteria and configuration rules. Relations define parametric equations and dependencies between dimensions, features, and parameters, and unwanted relations may introduce unnecessary complexity, incorrect design behavior, regeneration instability, or conflicts with standard modeling practices. Excess or inappropriate relations can make models difficult to maintain, troubleshoot, and modify, and may also lead to unintended changes in geometry or downstream errors in drawings, manufacturing, and simulation. Not permitted relations are typically defined in external ModelCHECK configuration files such as the start.mcs file or site-specific relation rule lists, where allowed and disallowed expressions, keywords, and naming patterns are specified based on company standards and modeling governance. Relation filtering rules and validation behavior are controlled by the REL_UNWANTED settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N Y Y

Check.Name: Sheet Metal Feature Bend Table
Check.Description: Checks whether the sheet metal bend table used for flat pattern generation in sheet metal parts adheres to predefined company standards and manufacturing requirements. The bend table defines critical parameters such as bend allowances, K-factors, and material-specific deformation behavior, which directly affect the accuracy of flat patterns used for manufacturing and downstream processes. Incorrect or nonstandard bend table definitions can lead to inaccurate flat patterns, forming issues, incorrect part dimensions, and manufacturing defects. This check helps ensure that sheet metal bend tables are correctly defined, consistent, and aligned with approved engineering and manufacturing standards. Bend table definitions and sheet metal rule settings are typically managed through Creo sheet metal configuration files and ModelCHECK settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SHTMTL_FBENDTAB settings in the check.mch configuration file..
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N Y Y

Check.Name: Sheet Metal K Factor
Check.Description: Checks whether the K-factor used in sheet metal parts is correctly defined and complies with predefined company standards and manufacturing guidelines.
