The K-factor controls how sheet metal material stretches during bending and directly affects bend allowance, flat pattern accuracy, and final part dimensions. Incorrect or inconsistent K-factor values can lead to inaccurate flat patterns, assembly fit issues, forming defects, and downstream manufacturing and inspection problems. This check helps ensure that sheet metal K-factor values are consistent, properly defined, and aligned with approved material and process standards. K-factor definitions and sheet metal rules are typically managed through Creo sheet metal configuration settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the SHTMTL_KFACTOR settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N Y Y

Check.Name: Create View
Check.Description: Checks whether standard views defined by company drawing or model templates are present in the model or drawing and ensures they conform to predefined view creation settings. Standard views typically include default orientations such as front, top, right, isometric, and other required engineering or manufacturing views that support consistent documentation and downstream usage. Missing or inconsistent views can lead to incomplete drawings, reduced clarity, and errors in manufacturing, inspection, and communication. Although this check can also be configured to automatically generate missing views, its primary purpose is validation against company standards to ensure consistency and completeness of view definitions. View creation rules and standard view definitions are typically managed through Creo drawing setup files and ModelCHECK configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the CREATE_VIEW settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N Y Y

Check.Name: Non Standard Scaled Drawing Views
Check.Description: Checks whether drawing view scales comply with predefined company standards and validates that all views use acceptable, controlled scale values appropriate for consistent documentation and manufacturing interpretation. Nonstandard or inconsistent view scaling can lead to misinterpretation of geometry, unclear drawings, inspection errors, and downstream manufacturing issues, especially when views are scaled arbitrarily or inconsistently within the same drawing. This check helps ensure that all drawing views use approved scale values that maintain clarity, consistency, and compliance with drafting standards. View scaling rules and acceptable scale definitions are typically managed through Creo drawing setup files and ModelCHECK configuration settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the VALIDATE_VIEW_SCALE settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y Y

Check.Name: Report Layers
Check.Description: Checks whether required layers are present in the model and reports any missing layers according to predefined company layer standards and configuration rules. Layers are used to organize model entities such as dimensions, annotations, datums, and geometry for clarity, control, and downstream processes like drawing management and manufacturing output. Missing or nonstandard layers can lead to inconsistent model organization, difficulty in data management, and issues in visualization or plotting. This check helps ensure that all required layers exist and conform to company layer naming and structure standards. Layer requirements and validation rules are typically defined through Creo layer configuration settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the REPT_LAYR_ALWAYS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N Y Y

Check.Name: Annotation on Multiple Combined States
Check.Description: Checks for annotations that exist across multiple combined states in the model and verifies that they remain consistent, properly managed, and correctly displayed in each applicable state. Annotations used in combined states (such as simplified representations, configurations, or display states) must remain aligned with design intent to ensure that critical information such as dimensions, notes, GDT, and manufacturing instructions is not missing, duplicated, or inconsistently shown across states. Inconsistencies can lead to incomplete documentation, misinterpretation during manufacturing or inspection, and downstream data quality issues. This check helps ensure that annotations are correctly controlled and consistently represented across all combined states of the model. Combined state annotation rules and display management are typically governed by Creo annotation and representation settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the ANNTN_ON_MULT_COMB_STATE settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y

Check.Name: Annotations with incorrect surface references
Check.Description: Checks whether annotation semantic reference text in the model is valid, properly defined, and correctly linked to its referenced model entities such as geometry, features, or parameters. Semantic references are used to maintain associative annotation behavior, ensuring that annotations update correctly when the underlying model changes. Invalid, broken, or missing semantic reference text can result in lost associativity, incorrect annotation placement, or outdated manufacturing and inspection information, which can compromise model-based definition integrity and downstream processes. This check helps ensure that all semantic references are correctly maintained, functional, and consistent with the current model state. Semantic reference validation rules and annotation associativity behavior are typically managed through Creo annotation and MBD settings, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the ANNTN_SEM_REF_TEXT_VERIFY settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y

Check.Name: Body Information
Check.Description: Checks whether body information in the model is valid, complete, and properly maintained according to company modeling and data management standards. This includes verifying that solid bodies are correctly defined, properly named, and have consistent metadata and references required for downstream processes such as manufacturing, simulation, mass property calculations, and model-based definition workflows. Missing, incorrect, or inconsistent body information can lead to errors in analysis, incorrect physical properties, and confusion in multibody part management. This check helps ensure that all bodies within the model are properly defined, traceable, and aligned with design intent. Body information rules and validation behavior are typically managed through Creo part modeling settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the BODY_INFO settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N Y

Check.Name: Combined State Name
Check.Description: Checks whether combined state names in the model follow the required company naming conventions and are consistently defined across the model. Combined states are used to control display configurations such as simplified representations, annotations, views, and model settings, and inconsistent or nonstandard naming can lead to confusion, poor data management, incorrect configuration usage, and downstream issues in documentation, manufacturing, and PLM systems. This check helps ensure that all combined states are properly named, standardized, and aligned with company-defined naming rules for clarity and consistency. Combined state naming rules and validation behavior are typically managed through Creo representation settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the COMB_STATE_NAME settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y

Check.Name: Ignore Annotation
Check.Description: Checks whether annotations marked to be ignored are properly excluded from ModelCHECK evaluations and ensures that only approved annotation exclusions are applied according to company standards. This allows certain annotations to be intentionally bypassed during checking to avoid unnecessary warnings, but improper or excessive use of ignore settings can hide valid issues, reduce model quality control, and lead to incomplete validation of critical design information. This check helps ensure that ignored annotations are intentional, controlled, and compliant with predefined rules for annotation management. Annotation exclusion rules and validation behavior are typically managed through Creo annotation settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the IGNORE_ANNTN settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N Y

Check.Name: Layer Saved Status
Check.Description: Checks the saved status of layers in the model to ensure that all layer configurations are properly stored, up to date, and consistent with the current model state. Layers control the organization of model entities such as geometry, annotations, dimensions, and datums, and unsaved or out-of-date layer states can lead to inconsistencies between the displayed model and its stored configuration, causing errors in downstream documentation, manufacturing, and data management processes. This check helps ensure that all layers are correctly saved and synchronized with the active model definition. Layer save status rules and validation behavior are typically managed through Creo layer management settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the LAYER_SAVED_STATUS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y

Check.Name: Unwanted Layers
Check.Description: Checks for non-standard or unwanted layers in the model and verifies that only approved layers defined by company standards are used to maintain a clean, organized, and efficient modeling structure. Unwanted layers may arise from legacy modeling practices, ad hoc user creation, or incorrect layer management, and can lead to confusion, inconsistent data organization, and difficulties in downstream processes such as drawing creation, annotation control, and manufacturing output. This check helps ensure that all layers in the model comply with predefined naming and usage rules and that no extraneous or nonconforming layers are present. Layer naming conventions and validation rules are typically managed through Creo layer settings and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the LAYER_UNWANTED settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YN Y Y N Y

Check.Name: Part Density
Check.Description: Checks whether the density assigned to the part model is correctly defined and compliant with the specified material properties and company-approved material standards. Part density is a critical property used for accurate mass calculations, inertia properties, simulation results, and downstream processes such as manufacturing, costing, and engineering analysis, and incorrect or missing density values can lead to significant errors in weight estimation and functional evaluation of the model. This check helps ensure that the assigned density matches the intended material definition and remains consistent with approved material libraries and engineering requirements. Material density validation rules and material property standards are typically managed through Creo material definitions and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the PRT_DENSITY settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW E E N Y

Check.Name: Template Layers
Check.Description: Checks whether template layers in the model conform to the expected company standards, including predefined layer names, structure, and usage rules defined in the model template. Template layers provide a standardized foundation for organizing model entities such as geometry, annotations, dimensions, and datums, and inconsistencies can lead to poor model organization, confusion in downstream drawing creation, and failures in maintaining company-wide modeling standards. This check helps ensure that all template layers are correctly defined, properly named, and consistent with approved layer management practices. Template layer definitions and validation rules are typically managed through Creo template models and ModelCHECK configuration files, while check behavior and reporting are controlled by the TEMPLATE_LAYERS settings in the check.mch configuration file.
Check.ModelCheckOptions: YNEW W W N Y
